Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, June 6, 2026

9:30 am - 11:30 am Artificial Intelligence - a Prologue for Osher's 2026 Summer Lecture Series Auditorium E, Dartmouth Hitchcock Medical Center One Medical Center Drive, Lebanon, NH 03766

Dr. G. Christian Jernstedt, Professor Emeritus of Psychological and Brain Sciences at Dartmouth, will offer a foundational lecture on Artificial Intelligence as an introduction to the 2026 Osher at Dartmouth Summer Lecture Series, which runs from July 8 through August 12.

Whether you’re planning on attending the Summer Lecture Series or not, this lecture will provide a down-to-earth discussion of what AI is and is not. As Dr. Jernstedt stated, “We’ll look behind the headlines, starting with the physically nearby origins of ‘AI.’ We’ll encounter extraordinary things AI can do today and uncover where dangers are hidden. The core ideas that will be discussed will prepare us to more fully understand the sessions of the Summer Lecture Series, which will address the question, "How is Artificial Intelligence Transforming America?”

Chris Jernstedt has been involved with AI since he was an undergraduate in electronic and computer engineering at Carnegie Mellon. He has lectured around the world on the core neuroscience that undergirds modern AI applications. His two recent lectures on AI at Kendal at Hanover were very well received. One Kendal resident remarked that it was the best lecture he had ever attended, on any topic.

COST:
$10 Osher at Dartmouth members
$20 non-members

Tickets are available for in-person.

REGISTER for in-person

Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth

1:00pm US@250: Big Watch Film Screening & Discussion Baker Free Library 509 South Street Bow NH 03304

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?


Interested in watching the entire film series? Learn more here: The American Revolution | All Episodes Now Streaming | Ken Burns | PBS


Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS, with support from Cogswell Benevolent Trust. 

Corporate funding for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by Bank of America. Major funding was provided by The Better Angels Society and its members Jeannie and Jonathan Lavine with the Crimson Lion Foundation; and the Blavatnik Family Foundation. Major funding was also provided by David M. Rubenstein; The Robert D. and Patricia E. Kern Family Foundation; Lilly Endowment Inc.; and the 
following Better Angels Society members: Eric and Wendy Schmidt; Stephen A. Schwarzman; and Kenneth C. Griffin with Griffin Catalyst. Additional support for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by: The Arthur Vining Davis Foundations; The Pew Charitable Trusts; Gilbert S. Omenn and Martha A. Darling; Park Foundation; and the following Better Angels Society members: Gilchrist and Amy Berg; 
Perry and Donna Golkin; The Michelson Foundation; Jacqueline B. Mars; Kissick Family Foundation; Diane and Hal Brierley; John H. N. Fisher and Jennifer Caldwell; John and Catherine Debs; The Fullerton Family Charitable Fund; Philip I. Kent; Gail Elden; Deborah and Jon Dawson; David and Susan Kreisman; The McCloskey Family Charitable Trust; Becky and Jim Morgan; Carol and Ned Spieker; Mark A. Tracy; 
and Paul and Shelley Whyte. THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was made possible, in part, with support from the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.

By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community-driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
4 pm - 5 pm Nona Hendryx: Cyboracle in the Nonaverse Daryl Roth Studio Theater

Fall into the Nonaverse, an immersive mixed-reality world where you'll be sung to and spellbound by Cyboracle, the Afro-futurist avatar of Nona Hendryx.

From the boundless mind of composer and transdisciplinary artist Nona Hendryx, these two interactive experiences will tease the senses and open your world to the creative horizons unlocked by new technologies.

From her early years as a founding member of Labelle, Hendryx has been stretching the limits of musical genre, audience engagement and Black thought. Now, following a premiere at Lincoln Center, she and her marvelous team of creative and technical collaborators bring us two paired experiences that transport us into the Nonaverse. 

Hendryx is the 2026 Roth Visiting Scholar.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

Jun 6 – Jun 6, 2026 NextGen Launch Party

The NextGen Circle is an opportunity for young people in the Upper Valley to connect over a love for the arts! Through discounted tickets and community events, we will provide a unique experience that opens up Northern Stage to young community members in a new way.

Dress for the masquerade and enjoy dancing, a cash bar, and light bites as we bring together young members of our community and introduce you to the NextGen Circle.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, June 7, 2026

2:00pm A Brewing Conflict Hopkinton Town Library Slusser Center Contoocook NH 03229

250 years ago. America is on the verge of its first civil war. Will you stand for Loyalty or Liberty? Or perhaps… Neutrality? Join the crew of Historic Pursuits as we invite you into a colonial coffeehouse, replete with music, costume, trivia, and of course, tasty beverages! Community theatre with the perfect blend of history and hilarity.  Feeling an urge to flex your First Amendment rights? We’ll be happy to outfit you in colonial garb and welcome you into the script! Want to exercise your right to remain silent and ponder a good cuppa brew? We've got you covered. Either way, be ready to immerse yourself into the food, music, drama and excitement of The American Revolution.

Sponsored by Hopkinton Town Library, Hopkinton Rotary, and Hopkinton Historical Society.

Registration requested, as space is limited. To register, contact Sarah Dangelas Hofe, Hopkinton Town Library, 603-746-3663

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchHopkinton Town Library and Hopkinton RotarySarah Dangelas Hofe info@hopkintontownlibrary.org

Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Songs of Old New Hampshire Clark Museum Complex 233 S. Main Street Wolfeboro NH 03894

Drawing heavily on the repertoire of traditional singer Lena Bourne Fish (1873-1945) of Jaffrey and Temple, NH, Jeff Warner offers the songs and stories that, in the words of Carl Sandburg, tell us "where we came from and what brought us along." These ballads, love songs and comic pieces, reveal the experiences and emotions of daily life in the days before movies, sound recordings and, for some, books. Songs from the lumber camps, the decks of sailing ships, the textile mills, and the war between the sexes offer views of pre-industrial New England and a chance to hear living artifacts from the 18th and 19th centuries. 

Humanities to GoJeff WarnerWolfeboro Historical SocietyLibby Shannon, wolfeborohistoricalsociety@gmail.com

7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Campton Historical Society Museum 529 NH Rt 175 Campton NH 03223

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Campton Historical Society Museum 529 NH Rt 175 Campton NH 03223

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsCampton Historical SocietyNancy Mardin mardinnancy@gmail.com

12 pm - 1 pm Dartmouth Cancer Grand Rounds-R.Singh Auditorium E, DH

Hosts:  SGN and Dr. 'toyin Asojo

MCB Graduate Program
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12 pm - 1 pm DCC Grand Rounds with Rakesh K. Singh, PhD DHMC, Auditorium E

Rakesh Singh, PhD, holds the department's Samuel M. and Janet L. Cohen Distinguished Professor in Pathology and Microbiology, where he also serves as vice chair of graduate education at the University of Nebraska Medical Center. His research focus is to understand the role of the tumor microenvironment in progression and metastasis.

Dr. Singh earned a bachelor's degree with honors in zoology in 1985, a master's of zoology in 1987, and a PhD in zoology in 1991, all from Banaras Hindu University in Varanasi, India.

He completed postdoctoral training at the University of Texas M.D. Anderson Cancer Center in Houston. He joined UNMC in 1995.

Dartmouth Cancer Center
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Unpacking the Declaration of Independence: What It Meant Then and Now Fuller Public Library 29 School St Hillsboro NH 03244

Learn about the text of the Declaration of Independence, who the key players were, the Enlightenment influence and if the values espoused in the Declaration are still relevant today. Participants will experience an unbiased look at the Declaration and receive valuable content to understand the influences and ideals of the document. They will be able to critically think about the document and have the knowledge of what it actually says. They will be able to form opinions and speak with conviction about what they learned.

Pre-registration, call the library 603-464-3595. Parking is across the street. Handicapped access in the rear of the building, elevator available.
Humanities to GoDavid AlcoxHillsborough Historical SocietyKeneth Matthews, 603-478-3814
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Ken Burns & The American Idea Capitol Center for the Arts 44 S. Main Street Concord NH 03301

Join New Hampshire PBS, New Hampshire Humanities and the New Hampshire Historical Society on June 9 at the Capitol Center for the Arts for Ken Burns and the American Idea – an inspiring evening with one of America’s most influential storytellers.

The show begins at 7:00 pm.

Hosted by award-winning journalist and author Laura Knoy, this event will feature a thoughtful conversation with Ken Burns about the people, events and enduring ideals that shape the American story.

The evening will also feature powerful excerpts from several of Burns’s landmark films, including his newest series, The American Revolution, bringing to life the stories, struggles and triumphs that helped define our nation.

Don’t miss this extraordinary opportunity to experience the power of history, storytelling and the American spirit – through the lens of one of the country’s most celebrated filmmakers.

Tickets to the general public will go on sale on April 2 at www.ccanh.com.

250th EventsNew Hampshire PBSCapitol Center for the Arts, 603-224-1111
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Wednesday, June 10, 2026

10:00am Discovering New England Stone Walls Penacook Community Room 76 Community Drive Penacook NH 03303

Why are we so fascinated with stone walls? Kevin Gardner, author of The Granite Kiss, explains how and why New England came to acquire its thousands of miles of stone walls, the ways that they and other dry stone structures were built, how their styles emerged and changed over time, and their significance to the famous New England landscape. Along the way, Kevin occupies himself building a miniature wall or walls on a tabletop, using tiny stones from a five-gallon bucket. 

Humanities to GoKevin GardnerConcord Parks & Recreation Senior ProgramLizzie Loughridge, llloughridge@concordnh.gov

4:30 pm - 5:30 pm Grief Group Collective monthly meeting 105 South Fairbanks Hall

This monthly bereavement group is for Dartmouth faculty, staff, postdocs & graduate students navigating their grief.  Each month we gather to support each other as well as learn something new to help us understand grief in new ways.  Please fill out this form (https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSfuttHmdqNbCwRDPEFvE7F3zYU7CUDvdLDNjKTaHoi_sGtmUA/viewform?_hsenc=p2ANqtz-81zzXjW-lXtc-yszT_LxpdXjeUGMK0YX0Jr773e6KQ6-55h_pc4BM0zRFibNpV8DajhjAzmzb30I8ugOxMaFSScqUobE4TDw6tXjOtZoHCIC-zNNQ&_hsmi=253870156) if you are new to the group.  Feel free to reach out to Rev. Nancy Vogele, College Chaplain, for more information.

Location: South Fairbanks Hall, room 105 (white building behind South Mass dorm and next to 53 Commons).
Tucker Center
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
5:30pm US@250: Big Watch Film Screening & Discussion Gilford Public Library 31 Potter Hill Rd Gilford NH 03249

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?


Interested in watching the entire film series? Learn more here: The American Revolution | All Episodes Now Streaming | Ken Burns | PBS


Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS, with support from Cogswell Benevolent Trust. 

Corporate funding for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by Bank of America. Major funding was provided by The Better Angels Society and its members Jeannie and Jonathan Lavine with the Crimson Lion Foundation; and the Blavatnik Family Foundation. Major funding was also provided by David M. Rubenstein; The Robert D. and Patricia E. Kern Family Foundation; Lilly Endowment Inc.; and the 
following Better Angels Society members: Eric and Wendy Schmidt; Stephen A. Schwarzman; and Kenneth C. Griffin with Griffin Catalyst. Additional support for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by: The Arthur Vining Davis Foundations; The Pew Charitable Trusts; Gilbert S. Omenn and Martha A. Darling; Park Foundation; and the following Better Angels Society members: Gilchrist and Amy Berg; 
Perry and Donna Golkin; The Michelson Foundation; Jacqueline B. Mars; Kissick Family Foundation; Diane and Hal Brierley; John H. N. Fisher and Jennifer Caldwell; John and Catherine Debs; The Fullerton Family Charitable Fund; Philip I. Kent; Gail Elden; Deborah and Jon Dawson; David and Susan Kreisman; The McCloskey Family Charitable Trust; Becky and Jim Morgan; Carol and Ned Spieker; Mark A. Tracy; 
and Paul and Shelley Whyte. THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was made possible, in part, with support from the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.

By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community-driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

 

We have upper and lower parking lots with Handicap Accessibility. The upper lot has an elevator as well.

Big WatchGilford Public LibraryPatty Myers, patty@gilfordlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm The Capital Crime of Witchcraft: What the Primary Sources Tell Us Throwback Brewery 7 Hobbs Road North Hampton NH 03862

On first impression, the witchcraft trials of the Colonial era may seem to have been nothing but a free-for-all, fraught with hysterics. Margo Burns explores an array of prosecutions in seventeenth century New England, using facsimiles of primary source manuscripts, from first formal complaints to arrest warrants, indictments of formal charges to death warrants, and the reversals of attainder and rescinding of excommunications years after the fact; demonstrating how methodically and logically the Salem Court worked. This program focuses on the Salem witchcraft trials of 1692 and 1693, when nineteen people were hanged and one crushed to death, but also examines a variety of other cases against women in New Hampshire, Massachusetts, and Connecticut. 

Free parking Held in collaboration with Throwback Brewery Food and drink available for purchase, but no purchase necessary to attend the event 18+ Requires registration at tinyurl.com/bdx8ktjt
Humanities to GoMargo BurnsNorth Hampton Public Library and Cultural CenterEmily Dolan, programming.nhpl@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:30pm What's The History of Your House? Chamberlin Free Public Library 46 Main St. Greenville NH 03048

The best thing about tracking down the history of a house is that your research subject usually stays in one place! House history projects give us the opportunity to access local historical resources such as newspapers, maps, deeds, city directories, and more. This avenue of research allows us to uncover architecture, neighborhoods, and people of the past. Join us as we track the records of a Masonic Lodge and delve into the history that is underfoot each day.

Free on-street parking.
Humanities to GoErin MoultonChamberlin Free Public LibraryCourtney, courtney@chamberlinlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Augustus Saint-Gaudens and the Cornish Colony Madison Library 11895 Village Road Madison NH 03849

By the early 20th century, Cornish, NH, in the shadow of Mount Ascutney, had become a summer haven for artists, writers, architects, musicians and other creative people. At the center of it all was renowned American sculptor, Augustus Saint-Gaudens. While the respite from city life and the area's natural beauty was appealing, even more so was the opportunity for creative people to enjoy each other's company, to share their ideas, to work hard and play hard, all within a days journey to New York and Boston. Explore the art and life of Augustus Saint-Gaudens, and learn about some of the creative artists, writers, architects and others who were drawn to the area.

Refreshments will be provided.
Humanities to GoInez McDermottFriends of Madison LibraryMary Lucy or Sloane Jarell, 603-367-4535 or 603-367-8545
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:30pm Galileo Galilei, the Starry Messenger Franklin Public Library 310 Central St. Franklin NH 03235

The Starry Messenger, presented by Michael Francis, is a dramatic fun-filled adaptation of Galileo's short treatise "Siderius Nuncius." Galileo (dressed in 17th-century costume) arrives to present a public lecture on his most recent discoveries made using his newly-devised spyglass. As he describes those discoveries, Galileo's new method of observation and measurement of nature become apparent. Throughout the presentation audience members are actively involved in experiments and demonstrations. After the lecture, Galileo answers questions about his experiments, his life, and his times. 

Located in the function room on the second floor. Handicap accessible from the parking lot behind the building. The event will take place following the Annual Member Meeting of Franklin Opera House, Inc.
Humanities to GoMichael FrancisFranklin Opera HouseFranklin Opera House, info@franklinoperahouse.org

Saturday, June 13, 2026

9 am - 10 am Undergraduate Commencement Rehearsal audience: Students-Undergraduate Leede Arena

During this required rehearsal, students will receive instructions and ‘what to know’ for Sunday’s ceremony, including their participation order number, the Commencement procession, and where they will sit on the Green. The Undergraduate Commencement Readers will review name pronunciations and check directly with students for assistance, as needed. We will rehearse the Commencement procession as we move to the Bema for Class Day.

Rehearsal attendance is limited to Commencement participants only.

If you are an AB/BE graduate, you will not be able to attend Commencement rehearsal due to Thayer Investiture. You will receive separate instructions.

Commencement participants who wish to discuss or request disability- or injury-related adjustments should contact the Undergraduate Deans Office at (603) 646-2243 or Undergraduate Deans Office. Requests can be made at any time, but some types of adjustments take several weeks to arrange.  Therefore, please request adjustments as soon as possible.

(Required - enter your event description here)

Comparative Literature Program
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
10:15 am - 12:00 pm Senior Class Day Exercises, followed by Class Photo Bema

Immediately following the Commencement rehearsal, seniors will process to the Bema for Class Day, entering the Bema at approximately 10:00 a.m. The program includes speeches by seniors and faculty, as well as an awards ceremony. Guests are invited to join the graduates at this event.

The listed end time of 12:00 p.m. is approximate - the event could end earlier or later.

Class Day will take place at the Bema in the event of rain, so plan accordingly.

The Bema is located at the end of a wide gravel path that branches off Observatory Road behind North Fayerweather. Parking is very limited near the Bema, so guests should plan their travel time to accommodate walking to the event. The space is an outdoor amphitheater with a limited number of chairs for guests, available on a first come, first served basis. Since many guests will need to sit on the ground, family and friends are encouraged to bring blankets to sit on. Students will sit together on the grass in an area in front of the stage. Much of the terrain is hilly and rocky; however the space is wheelchair accessible. We recommend guests with mobility disabilities arrive by 9:30 a.m.

Immediately following Class Day, the seniors will gather in front of Dartmouth Hall for a Class Photo.

Students who wish to discuss or request disability- or injury-related adjustments should contact the Undergraduate Deans Office at (603) 646-2243 or Undergraduate Deans Office . Requests can be made at any time, but some types of adjustments take several weeks to arrange. Therefore, please request adjustments as soon as possible.

Dean of the College
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
11 am - 1 pm Baker Library Bell Tower Tour Baker Library - Berry Main Street

For Additional information, visit the Baker Library Bell Tower website.

Reaching the Bell Tower catwalk requires climbing several flights of stairs, including a “ladder-like” stair for the last 20 feet. Climbing the stairs and walking outside on the catwalk may be physically demanding and difficult for any person uncomfortable with heights.

Groups of nine are brought up to the tower in 20-minute intervals.

Signups take place at the entrance to the Baker stacks on the first floor of Berry on a first-come, first-served basis. Signups begin 30 minutes prior to tours in person only, and spots will fill up quickly.

11 am - 12 pm Cognitive Science Program End of Year Celebration Anonymous Hall C400 Rooftop

Please join us in celebrating our graduating seniors. Family and friends are welcome.

Cognitive Science Program
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
11:00am Music in My Pockets: Family Fun in Folk Music Rodgers Memorial Library 194 Derry Rd Hudson NH 03051

Singing games, accessible "pocket instruments" like spoons and dancing puppets, tall tales, funny songs, old songs and songs kids teach each other in the playground-all "traditional" in that they have been passed down the generations by word of mouth-will be seen, heard and learned. We will revisit 1850 or 1910 in a New England town, with families gathered around the kitchen hearth, participating in timeless, hearty entertainment: a glimpse into how America amused itself before electricity. This program is recommended for adults and children ages 6 and above. 

Humanities to GoJeff WarnerFriends of the LibraryCarrie Loring, events@rodgerslibrary.org

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?

Add to Google Calendar
8:15 am - 9:00 am Commencement Assembly of Seniors Leede Arena

Undergraduate Commencement participants assemble by 8:15 a.m. - wearing their caps and gowns (academic regalia) and must be in their assigned seats by 8:30 a.m. The doors will be closed promptly at 8:30 a.m. in order to prepare the final participant order. Any student arriving after 8:30 a.m. will process at the end of the procession. At a signal from the undergraduate dean, the class marshals and the bagpiper will lead the Seniors to the Green. Once on the Green, the Seniors will form two lines through which the Academic Procession will advance toward the platform, providing an opportunity for acknowledgement of the accomplishments of the Seniors and the support of faculty and administrators.

Attendance is limited to graduating students and other undergraduate commencement participants only.

Commencement participants who wish to discuss or request disability- or injury-related adjustments should contact the Undergraduate Deans Office at (603) 646-2243 or Undergraduate Deans Office. Requests can be made at any time, but some types of adjustments take several weeks to arrange.  Therefore, please request adjustments as soon as possible.

Conferences and Events
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12:00pm Revolutionary Portsmouth Strawbery Banke Museum 14 Hancock Street Portsmouth NH 03801

Strawbery Banke Museum celebrates Flag Day June 14 with a free annual public event. This year, Strawbery Banke and Portsmouth Historical Society are collaborating on "Revolutionary Portsmouth" programming and Flag Day tells the story of the role of the Portsmouth Liberty Pole (erected in 1766 -- the first in the nation) and the original flag created by the Continental Congress and adopted on June 14, 1777 -- the same day Captain John Paul Jones took command of the Portsmouth-built "Ranger" which was the first to have the flag recognized by a foreign nation. This short, colorful, family-friendly program is followed immediately by the Ken Burns Big Watch program and "Spirit of Service" facilitated discussion.

This program is a partnership between Association of Historical Societies in NH, Portsmouth Historical Society, and Strawbery Banke Museum.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchAHSNH, Strawbery Banke, Portsmouth Historical Society, Portsmouth Public LibraryStephanie Seacord sseacord@lemd.com

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

Darling; Park Foundation; and the following Better Angels Society members: Gilchrist and Amy Berg; 
Perry and Donna Golkin; The Michelson Foundation; Jacqueline B. Mars; Kissick Family Foundation; Diane and Hal Brierley; John H. N. Fisher and Jennifer Caldwell; John and Catherine Debs; The Fullerton Family Charitable Fund; Philip I. Kent; Gail Elden; Deborah and Jon Dawson; David and Susan Kreisman; The McCloskey Family Charitable Trust; Becky and Jim Morgan; Carol and Ned Spieker; Mark A. Tracy; 
and Paul and Shelley Whyte. THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was made possible, in part, with support from the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.

By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community-driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

 

The screening takes place after the annual Flag Day ceremony (free) at Strawbery Banke Museum at 12 noon. There will be a children's flag-making activity between the end of that program and the screening. Film attendees receive free admission to the museum and its historic houses all day.

This program is a partnership between Association of Historical Societies in NH, Portsmouth Historical Society, and Strawbery Banke Museum.

Big WatchAHSNH, Strawbery Banke, Portsmouth Historical Society, Portsmouth Public LibraryStephanie Seacord, sseacord@lemd.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Monday, June 15, 2026

6:00pm Portraits of a Revolution Bethlehem Public Library 2245 Main St. Bethlehem NH 03574

This program explores American portraits painted and sculpted during the years leading up to, and the decades after, the Revolution. The ideals, values and virtues expressly stated in our founding documents are easily identifiable in these portraits once we know how to “read” them. We will examine portraits of founding “fathers” and “mothers,” as well as of lesser-known individuals, as documents that can be read like texts. We will also discuss the tradition of portraiture, and the influences of earlier art movements, artists, and artifacts, that inform these American portraits. Audiences will be introduced to, and practice, some skills of art analysis and learn how art works can reveal so much about a culture.

Everyone is welcome! Bethlehem Public Library is ADA Accessible and air conditioned. Parking in the library lot.
Humanities to GoInez McDermottBethlehem Public LibraryRebecca Goldstein, rgoldstein@BethlehemNHlibrary.gov
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm US@250: Big Watch Film Screening & Discussion Dover Public Library 73 Locust St. Dover NH 03820

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?


Interested in watching the entire film series? Learn more here: The American Revolution | All Episodes Now Streaming | Ken Burns | PBS


Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS, with support from Cogswell Benevolent Trust. 

Corporate funding for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by Bank of America. following Better Angels Society members: Eric and Wendy Schmidt; Stephen A. Schwarzman; and Kenneth C. Griffin with Griffin Catalyst. and Paul and Shelley Whyte. THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was made possible, in part, with support from the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.

Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, June 16, 2026

9 am - 3 pm Course Design Institute Day 1 - June 2026 audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Students-Graduate In person on campus

Dartmouth's Course Design Institute (CDI) provides a foundation in an integrated course design approach that is rooted in student-centered teaching and learning. Participants gain exposure to current theory of course design and, with support from a cohort of peers and guidance from CDI facilitators, apply evidence-based approaches to the designs of their own courses. This process can be used to develop a new course or redesign an existing course. This live institute will take place in person on campus June 16 and 18, 2026 from 9:00 am-3:00 pm.

Apply by May 18!

Faculty, Postdoc, Students-GraduateDartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL), Learning Design and Innovation

12 pm - 1 pm Dartmouth Cancer Center:David B. Kaner Memorial Lecture - A.Lowy Auditorium E, DH

Dartmouth Cancer Center:  David B. Kaner Memorial Lecture

Auditorium E, DH

Host:  Dr. John Mullen

MCB Graduate Program
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12 pm - 1 pm David B. Kaner Memorial Lecture with Andrew M. Lowy, MD DHMC Auditorium E

Dr. Lowy holds the Levine Family Chancellor’s Distinguished Chair in Surgical Oncology and is Professor of Surgery and Pharmacology, Director of Surgical Oncology and Associate Director of Moores Cancer Center at UC San Diego Health.  He is a physician-scientist who’s clinical and research primarily focuses on identifying and testing novel therapies for pancreatic and appendix cancer.  His laboratory co-developed the first mouse model of human pancreatic cancer which has become the standard in the field, has been licensed to pharma and used by hundreds of labs worldwide. The Lowy laboratory focuses on novel target identification, preclinical modeling and experimental therapeutics. Dr. Lowy’s research has led to the development and testing of novel therapies which have reached the clinic, most recently a tumor penetrating peptide which is being tested in randomized Phase 2 trials and the identification of GNAS mutation as a biomarker for the repurposing of palbociclib in the management of GNAS mutant mucinous cancer.

Dartmouth Cancer Center
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
2:00pm Unpacking the Declaration of Independence: What It Meant Then and Now Bedford Public Library 3 Meetinghouse Road Bedford NH 03110

Learn about the text of the Declaration of Independence, who the key players were, the Enlightenment influence and if the values espoused in the Declaration are still relevant today. Participants will experience an unbiased look at the Declaration and receive valuable content to understand the influences and ideals of the document. They will be able to critically think about the document and have the knowledge of what it actually says. They will be able to form opinions and speak with conviction about what they learned.

Humanities to GoDavid AlcoxBedford Public LibraryBedford Public Library, bedfordnhlibrary.org, reference@bedfordnh.gov, 603-472-2300
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:30pm A Walk Back in Time: The Secrets of Cellar Holes Salisbury Historical Society 655 Old Turnpike Road Salisbury NH 03268

Northern New England is full of reminders of past lives: stone walls, old foundations, a century-old lilac struggling to survive as the forest reclaims a once-sunny dooryard. What forces shaped settlement, and later abandonment, of these places? Adair Mulligan explores the rich story to be discovered in what remains behind. See how one town has set out to create an inventory of its cellar holes, piecing together the clues in the landscape. Such a project can help landowners know what to do if they have archaeological sites on their land and help stimulate interest in a town's future through its past. 

Humanities to GoAdair MulliganSalisbury Historical SocietyJoseph Schmidl, President@SalisburyHistoricalSociety.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Wednesday, June 17, 2026

5:30pm Live Free or Dial Silsby Free Public Library 226 Main St. Charlestown NH 03603

Explore the history of telecommunications with The New Hampshire Telephone Museum! This program is adaptable for both youth and adult audiences:

Youth programs: The New Hampshire Telephone Museum Storytime! There are three stories to choose from - the telephone, sound waves, or the telegraph.  Read a story with our educator, make a Bee Buzzer, thumbprint birds, or a morse code bracelet, play a game, explore vintage phones, telegraphs, and other hands-on activities. 

Adult program: The New Hampshire Telephone Museum houses a tangible history of telecommunications with over 1,000 artifacts. This presentation will highlight important moments in telephone history, such as the race to the patent office, the switchboard and party lines, and the undertaker who invented the dial system. People will be able to watch a Powerpoint tour around the museum and handle vintage phones from different time periods including a candlestick, rotary, and liquid telephone to name a few.

This event will be at the library, and parking is conveniently located around the back, side, and front of the building.
Humanities to GoMegan HurleySilsbury Free Public LibraryJenise Sileo, 6038267793

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Griffin Free Public Library 22 Hooksett Rd Auburn NH 03032

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Preregistration required - email Staff@griffinfree.org.
Humanities to GoMary AdamsGriffin Free Public LibraryMary Hrubiec, Staff@griffinfree.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Who Was John Stark? Freedom Town Hall 16 Elm Street Freedom NH 03836

While many are familiar with the state motto “live free or die,” few might know about the man, General John Stark, who uttered these words. In this presentation, George Morrison reveals the man – a ransomed captive, Ranger officer, road-builder, lumberman, husband and father, repeatedly called-on to serve but often clashing with authority -- whose phrase has become synonymous with our state. 

Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonFreedom Historical SocietySteve Thurston, 603-491-8347

6:30pm Women of '76 Meredith Public Library 91 Main Street Meredith NH 03253

A Theatrical Tribute to Lesser Known Women of the American Revolution
Step back in time and discover the untold stories of 5 courageous women, both patriot and loyalist, American and foreign born,  who witnessed, chronicled and in some cases changed the course of the American Revolution.

Presented by Rita Parisi.

Friday, June 19, 2026

10 am - 11 am Microbiology and Immunology Thesis Seminar - N.Kordana academic Chilcott Auditorium and Zoom

https://dartmouth.zoom.us/j/95500186877?pwd=JLnoBEyrxlMdAkqQbVE9ytrPIz0NyI.1

Advisor:  Robert Cramer

MCB Graduate Program
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
12 pm - 1 pm DCC Special Grand Rounds with Orazio Vittorio, PhD DHMC Auditorium G

 

Orazio Vittorio is an internationally recognized cancer biologist and Scientia Associate Professor at the University of New South Wales (UNSW), Sydney, where he leads the Metal Targeted Therapy & Immunology Laboratory. His research program is best known for pioneering work on copper metabolism in cancer, including the world-first demonstration that neuronal tumour cells accumulate significantly higher intracellular copper levels than non-malignant cells. This foundational discovery drove the development of novel copper-targeted therapeutics for neuroblastoma and gliomas, as well as a ⁶⁴CuCl₂-based PET imaging strategy that inspired GE Healthcare's PET imaging program for solid tumors.

His more recent work has focused on the role of copper in tumour immune evasion, with a seminal finding that copper regulates PD-L1 expression — establishing a new link between metal homeostasis and cancer immunology. Building on this, his lab identified a strategy to enhance the efficacy of anti-GD2 immunotherapy in neuroblastoma, published in Nature Communications (2024), which has since been incorporated into the international BEACON-2 clinical trial. He continues to investigate copper metabolism in aggressive childhood cancers including neuroblastoma, lower-grade glioma, DIPG, and glioblastoma, with a strong emphasis on translating laboratory discoveries into clinical practice.

Dartmouth Cancer Center
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Corbin’s Animal Garden South Danbury Christian Church 1411 Route 4 Danbury NH 03230

In the late 1800s, the banking, railroad, and real estate mogul Austin Corbin returned to his hometown in Newport, New Hampshire. He built a grand estate and bought out his neighbors’ farms to create an 22,000 acre wildlife game preserve stocked with boar, bison, bighorn sheep, antelope, elk, Chinese pheasant, and other imported animals. The grounds eventually became a prestigious private hunting park and hosted illustrious guests including Theodore Roosevelt, the Prince of Wales, Cornelius Vanderbilt, Joe DiMaggio, Rudyard Kipling, and Augustus Saint-Gaudens.

This illustrated slideshow features archival images and a discussion of the complicated history and legacy of New Hampshire’s own American Gilded Age robber baron. The talk will also highlight the important legacy of the role the Corbin family and park naturalist Ernest Baynes played in the saving of the American bison from extinction.

Free to all, plenty of parking, wheelchair assessable. Snacks and drinks provided.
Humanities to GoMary KronenwetterSouth Danbury Christian ChurchBonnie Nichols, bonnien222@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm New England Quilts and the Stories They Tell Chase’s Mill 801 Forest rd ( rt123) Alstead NH 03602

Quilts tell stories, and quilt history is full of myths and misinformation as well as heart-warming tales of service and tradition. Nearly every world culture that has cold weather uses quilted textiles – quilting is NOT just an American art. Pam Weeks weaves world history, women's history, industrial history and just plain wonderful stories into her presentation. Participants are invited to bring one quilt for identification and/or story sharing. Prompted in part by the material culture at hand, the presenter may speak about fashion fads, the Colonial Revival, quilt making for Civil War soldiers, and anything else quilt-related she can squeeze in.

The building is accessible, please pre-register, space and parking limited
Humanities to GoPamela WeeksChase’s MillJill Hall, Jhall@chasesmill.org,(508)821-8935
Full details ↗

7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Bradford Historical Society 162 East Main Street Bradford NH 03221

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

Saturday, June 20, 2026

11:00am Who Was John Stark? Daland Memorial Library 8 Lilac Lane Mont Vernon NH 03057

While many are familiar with the state motto “live free or die,” few might know about the man, General John Stark, who uttered these words. In this presentation, George Morrison reveals the man – a ransomed captive, Ranger officer, road-builder, lumberman, husband and father, repeatedly called-on to serve but often clashing with authority -- whose phrase has become synonymous with our state. 

Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonDaland Memorial LibraryBonnie Angulas, 6036737888
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
2:00pm Late in Arriving, How Electricity Changed Rural New Hampshire Life New Ipswich Library 6 Main Street New Ipswich NH 03071

Imagine a New Hampshire town where some people enjoyed the benefits of electricity – lighting at the flick of a switch and reliable heating controlled by a thermostat – while others lived with smelly kerosene lamps and smoky box stoves. In New Hampshire, during the first half of the 20th century,  residents of developed communities enjoyed the transformative benefits of electric power while those in the sparsely populated regions lived and worked in conditions little changed from the 19th century.  

It took the coming of the New Deal's Rural Electrification Administration and a determined band of farmers to overcome opposition from the established private utilities to create the New Hampshire Electric Cooperative in 1939. Despite labor and material shortages during World War II, within a decade, power came to almost all of the previously unserved areas of the state. For thousands of households, this meant relief from the drudgery of the wood-fueled cookstove, the washboard and the kerosene lamp. Reliable electric power similarly brought new ways of life to farmers and artisans. This program will explore how these developments changed civic and social life in New Hampshire’s countryside, and touch the ways the disparity of broadband access in the state today echoes these earlier struggles.

Parking is available along Main Street, in front and on the lawn of the New Ipswich Historical Society (23 Main), and in the small library parking lot. Light refreshments will be served.
Humanities to GoStephen TaylorNew Ipswich Historical SocietyMargaret Lee, margaretlee@myfairpoint.net
Full details ↗

2:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Webster Free Public Library 947 Battle Street Webster NH 03303

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, June 21, 2026

8:30 am - 6:45 pm "Les Misérables" on Broadway with Osher at Dartmouth Citizens Bank Opera House, 539 Washington St. Boston, MA 02111

Les Misérables remains the world’s most popular musical. Presented by Cameron Mackintosh, this acclaimed production of Alain Boublil and Claude-Michel Schönberg’s Tony Award®–winning musical phenomenon has captivated audiences around the globe. This powerful staging brings Victor Hugo’s timeless story to life. Set against the backdrop of 19th-century France, Les Misérables tells an epic tale of broken dreams, unrequited love, passion, sacrifice, and redemption—a moving testament to the resilience of the human spirit. Its magnificent score features beloved songs including “I Dreamed a Dream,” “On My Own,” “Bring Him Home,” “One Day More,” and “Master of the House.” Seen by more than 130 million people in 53 countries and 22 languages, Les Misérables stands as one of the most celebrated musicals in theatrical history.

Seats are locatied in the Mezzanine Left and Rights sides, Rows D-G, offering a great view of the stage.

The bus will depart from Lebanon at 8:30 AM, with a stop at the Exit 12 Park & Ride before continuing to Boston. We will be dropped off near the Citizens Bank Opera House, where you will have time to enjoy lunch on your own before the performance. The show runs from 1:00 PM to 4:00 PM. Following the performance, we will depart Boston by 4:30 PM, with an expected return home around 6:45 PM.

COST (includes bus and performance ticket):
$220.00 per Osher member
$245 per non-member

REGISTER

 

 

 

 

Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth

This program explores American portraits painted and sculpted during the years leading up to, and the decades after, the Revolution. The ideals, values and virtues expressly stated in our founding documents are easily identifiable in these portraits once we know how to “read” them. We will examine portraits of founding “fathers” and “mothers,” as well as of lesser-known individuals, as documents that can be read like texts. We will also discuss the tradition of portraiture, and the influences of earlier art movements, artists, and artifacts, that inform these American portraits. Audiences will be introduced to, and practice, some skills of art analysis and learn how art works can reveal so much about a culture.

12 pm - 1 pm Dartmouth Cancer Center Grand Rounds -N.Goel Auditorium E, DH

MCB Graduate Program
Full details ↗
Jun 23 Jun 30
12 pm - 1 pm DCC Grand Rounds with Neha Goel, MD, MPH DHMC Auditorium E

Neha Goel, MD, MPH is an Associate Attending on the faculty of the Breast Service, Department of Surgery, Chief of Disparities of the Department of Surgery at Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center, and the Jeanne A. Petrek Junior Faculty Chair. Dr. Goel is principal investigator of the ZIP Code to Genomic Code lab and leads a multi-institutional prospective epidemiologic cohort study to understand multilevel pathways of breast cancer disparities. She is funded by an NIH R01/R37 MERIT, the Breast Cancer Research Foundation, the Society of Surgical Oncology, and the American Society of Clinical Oncology and uses translational epidemiologic methods to understand how neighborhood disadvantage and social adversity influence cancer biology and outcomes.

Dartmouth Cancer Center

Kent; Gail Elden; Deborah and Jon Dawson; David and Susan Kreisman; The McCloskey Family Charitable Trust; Becky and Jim Morgan; Carol and Ned Spieker; Mark A. Tracy; 
and Paul and Shelley Whyte. THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was made possible, in part, with support from the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.

By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community-driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

 

Big WatchDurham Public LibraryDebbie Jarvis djarvis@ci.durham.nh.us
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Wednesday, June 24, 2026

12:30pm Corbin’s Animal Garden Bow Old Town Hall 91 Bow Center Rd Bow NH 03304

In the late 1800s, the banking, railroad, and real estate mogul Austin Corbin returned to his hometown in Newport, New Hampshire. He built a grand estate and bought out his neighbors’ farms to create an 22,000 acre wildlife game preserve stocked with boar, bison, bighorn sheep, antelope, elk, Chinese pheasant, and other imported animals. The grounds eventually became a prestigious private hunting park and hosted illustrious guests including Theodore Roosevelt, the Prince of Wales, Cornelius Vanderbilt, Joe DiMaggio, Rudyard Kipling, and Augustus Saint-Gaudens.

This illustrated slideshow features archival images and a discussion of the complicated history and legacy of New Hampshire’s own American Gilded Age robber baron. The talk will also highlight the important legacy of the role the Corbin family and park naturalist Ernest Baynes played in the saving of the American bison from extinction.

There is free parking and building is handicap accessible.

Humanities to GoMary KronenwetterBow Young at Heart ClubJudy Klotz 603-224-2428
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
3:00pm Live Free or Dial Olivia rodham memorial library 1 nelson common rd Nelson NH 03457

Explore the history of telecommunications with The New Hampshire Telephone Museum! This program is adaptable for both youth and adult audiences:

Youth programs: The New Hampshire Telephone Museum Storytime! There are three stories to choose from - the telephone, sound waves, or the telegraph.  Read a story with our educator, make a Bee Buzzer, thumbprint birds, or a morse code bracelet, play a game, explore vintage phones, telegraphs, and other hands-on activities. 

Adult program: The New Hampshire Telephone Museum houses a tangible history of telecommunications with over 1,000 artifacts. This presentation will highlight important moments in telephone history, such as the race to the patent office, the switchboard and party lines, and the undertaker who invented the dial system. People will be able to watch a Powerpoint tour around the museum and handle vintage phones from different time periods including a candlestick, rotary, and liquid telephone to name a few.

Humanities to GoMegan HurleyOlivia Rodham Memorial LibraryKris Finnegan, nelsonlibrary25@gmail.com
Full details ↗

This presentation will highlight important moments in telephone history, such as the race to the patent office, the switchboard and party lines, and the undertaker who invented the dial system. 5:30pm US@250: Big Watch Film Screening & Discussion Hooksett Public Library 31 Mt. St. Mary's Way Hooksett NH 03106

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?


Interested in watching the entire film series? Learn more here: The American Revolution | All Episodes Now Streaming | Ken Burns | PBS


Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS, with support from Cogswell Benevolent Trust.

following Better Angels Society members: Eric and Wendy Schmidt; Stephen A. Schwarzman; and Kenneth C. Griffin with Griffin Catalyst. 6:00pm Cozy Paint Night 18 Park Street 18 Park Street Northfield NH 03276

To celebrate the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence and the American Revolution, Hall Memorial Library and Lady Liberty’s Confectionery will host a special edition of our “Cozy Paint Night” program. In this hands-on art workshop, participants will create artwork inspired by the themes of the American Revolution with the help of Hannah!

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. Big WatchHall Memorial LibraryChelsea Bennett Chelsea@hallmemoriallibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution The Center at Eastman 6 Club House Lane Grantham NH 03753

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsEastman Community AssociationMary Niles, 603 863 7022
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Thursday, June 25, 2026

1:00pm War, Justice, and Non-Violence: Perspectives and Paradoxes Pembroke Town Library 313 Pembroke Street Pembroke NH 03275

How and why are wars fought? What exactly is a just war? This program looks at the history of "just war theory," starting in antiquity and following the development of three major elements of just war thinking: jus ad bellum (the right to war), jus in bello (the laws of war), and jus post bellum (justice after war). Highlighting the work of philosophers Larry May, Michael Walzer, and Richard Norman, Kent McConnell focuses discussion on the philosophical and theological foundations of just war thinking and non-violence. 

Humanities to GoKent McConnellFriends of the Pembroke Town LibraryRyan O'Hora, 603-485-7851
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
5:30pm Portraits of a Revolution Hooksett public library 31 mt saint marys way Hooksett NH 03106

This program explores American portraits painted and sculpted during the years leading up to, and the decades after, the Revolution. The ideals, values and virtues expressly stated in our founding documents are easily identifiable in these portraits once we know how to “read” them. We will examine portraits of founding “fathers” and “mothers,” as well as of lesser-known individuals, as documents that can be read like texts. We will also discuss the tradition of portraiture, and the influences of earlier art movements, artists, and artifacts, that inform these American portraits. Audiences will be introduced to, and practice, some skills of art analysis and learn how art works can reveal so much about a culture.

Accessible parking.
Humanities to GoInez McDermottHooksett historical societyjim sullivan, 603 315 0084
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm That Reminds Me of a Story NH Forest & Lands Building 629 Main Street Lancaster NH 03584

Stories speak to us of community. They hold our history and reflect our identity. Rebecca Rule has made it her mission over the last 20 years to collect stories of New Hampshire, especially those that reflect what's special about this rocky old place. She'll tell some of those stories – her favorites are the funny ones – and invite audience members to contribute a few stories of their own.

Humanities to GoRebecca RuleWeeks State Park AssociationBill Ghelli, skimtp@gmail.com 802-328-4147
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:30pm African American Soldiers and Sailors of New Hampshire During the American Revolution Chesterfield Public Library 524 Rt. 63 Chesterfield NH 03443

One of the most interesting aspects of the American Revolution is the role played by African Americans in the fight for independence. Both free African Americans and those that were enslaved were key in manning state militias and Continental Army units, as well as serving on the high seas in the Navy and on privately armed ships. Indeed, their service to the colonies was crucial in a conflict that lasted nearly seven years. Prohibited from serving in military units and largely considered "undesirable elements," how is it that these African-American soldiers came to fight for the cause of liberty, even when their own personal liberty was not guaranteed?

Glenn Knoblock examines the history of African-American soldiers' service during the war, including how and why they enlisted, their interaction with white soldiers, service on the battlefields, how they were perceived by the enemy and the officers under whom they served, and their treatment after the war. 

Humanities to GoGlenn KnoblockChesterfield Public LibraryMaddie McLaren, frontdesk@chesterfieldlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:30 pm - 9:30 pm Circa: Humans 2.0 Moore Theater, Hopkins Center for the Arts

All dates: Jun 25, Jun 26

Ten bodies. No limits.

They appear in a flash of light, moving in harmony for a fleeting moment and then descend into a sinuous trance. Towers grow and decay, bodies leap and are caught, as physical limits are pushed to their extreme. Can we ever find a perfect balance or is adapting to constant change the only way forward?

Circa's Humans wowed Hop audiences in 2019, and this thrilling next is a tightly woven choreography of bodies, pulsing with music by composer Ori Lichtik and revealed in Paul Jackson's dramatic lighting. Created by circus visionary Yaron Lifschitz and the Circa ensemble, Humans 2.0 is intimate, primal and deeply engaged with the challenge of being human.

Humans 2.0 is commissioned by The Mondavi Center, UC Davis

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Jun 25 Jun 26

Friday, June 26, 2026

5:00pm Humanities@Home: US@250 Big Watch Virtual Virtual Concord NHH 03301

Join us virtually to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion! Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more.
Expert facilitator Courtney Marshall, Ph.D, will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:
• What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future? 
• What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history? 
• How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?
This is a discussion, so come ready to participate!


Registration is required: CLICK TO REGISTER

Add to Google Calendar
11:00am Unpacking the Declaration of Independence: What It Meant Then and Now Sandown Public Library 305 Main Street Sandown NH 03873

Learn about the text of the Declaration of Independence, who the key players were, the Enlightenment influence and if the values espoused in the Declaration are still relevant today. Participants will experience an unbiased look at the Declaration and receive valuable content to understand the influences and ideals of the document. They will be able to critically think about the document and have the knowledge of what it actually says. They will be able to form opinions and speak with conviction about what they learned.

Humanities to GoDavid AlcoxSandown Public LibraryTricia Thomas, programming@sandownlibrary.us
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
11:00am Music Across Culture: Traditional African Drum and Dance Performed by the Akwaaba Ensemble Londonderry Town Common 265 Mammoth Road Londonderry NH 03053

Come to the Londonderry Town Common to hear the Akwaaba Traditional African Drum and Dance Ensemble perform! The group includes past members of the National Dance Ensemble, Brekete Ensemble and the Shidaa cultural troupe. The Akwaaba Ensemble’s energetic and engaging performances reflect their name, which means ‘welcome’ in the Twi language of the Ashanti tribe of Ghana. At each performance the Ensemble brings Highlife music, West African drumming and dance to vivid life. The goal of the group is to produce extraordinary music and great performances that enlighten the audience about African culture and music. The performance will be 45 minutes followed by a 15 minute discussion and Q&A portion. This program has been made possible through the Civic Life and Belonging Grant from the New Hampshire Humanities as well as the generous sponsorship of the Friends of the Library and the Friends of Music.

Civic Life and Belonging GrantsLeach LibraryBecca Berezin rberezin@londonderrynh.gov (603) 432-1132

Ten bodies. No limits.

They appear in a flash of light, moving in harmony for a fleeting moment and then descend into a sinuous trance. Towers grow and decay, bodies leap and are caught, as physical limits are pushed to their extreme. Can we ever find a perfect balance or is adapting to constant change the only way forward?

Circa's Humans wowed Hop audiences in 2019, and this thrilling next is a tightly woven choreography of bodies, pulsing with music by composer Ori Lichtik and revealed in Paul Jackson's dramatic lighting. 2:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Effingham Historical Society Building 1014 Province Lake Road Effingham NH 03882

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Program is free and open to the public. No registration required. Light refreshments will be served. Parking is available in the adjacent parking lot and along side streets.
Humanities to GoMary AdamsEffingham Historic District & Heritage CommissionSusan Slack, 603-724-3122

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 9:30 pm National Theatre in HD: The Playboy of the Western World Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Nicola Coughlan ('Bridgerton') and Siobhán McSweeney ('Derry Girls') join forces in this acclaimed Irish comedy about youth and self-discovery.

Pegeen Flaherty (Coughlin) has a sharp tongue, is frustrated with village life, works in a pub full of men, and yearns for something exciting to happen. She gets what she wished for when, on a normal day, a young man walks into her pub claiming that he's killed his father. Instead of being shunned, the killer Christy Mahon becomes a local hero, winning hearts and quickly embedding (emphasis on bedding) himself into village life. That is until a second man unexpectedly arrives on the scene…

Set in a remote Irish village, John Millington Synge's riveting story of youth and self-discovery was, at the time of its 1907 premiere, heavily criticized by its Irish audience for portraying the citizenry as drunk and morally lax while the country was seeking its independence. But since that time, this biting dark comedy has become one of the all-time classics of modernist Irish theatre, and the questions it raises about whose stories we believe and how we choose our heroes is interesting to revisit in our current moment.

Please note: This production contains graphic depictions of violence and injury, references to murder and violence, sexual references, complex family relationships and alcoholism.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗

3:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Stephenson Memorial Library 761 Forest Road Greenfield NH 03047

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else.
