Tuesday, June 30, 2026

12 pm - 1 pm DCC Grand Rounds with Sherry-Ann Brown, MD, PhD, FACC, FAHA DHMC, Auditorium E

Sherry-Ann Brown, MD, PhD, FACC, FAHA is a board-certified cardiologist and physician scientist specializing in preventive cardio-oncology, cancer survivorship, and the application of artificial intelligence to cardiovascular risk prediction. Dr Brown is a Clinical Associate Professor in the Community-Based Faculty Program at Florida International University College of Medicine. She is also the Founder of the Heart Innovation & Equity Research Institute. Dr. Brown leads national and internationally focused clinical, research, and digital innovation efforts at the intersection of cancer and cardiovascular disease. She is the first cardiologist licensed to practice medicine in all 50 U.S. states and Washington, D.C., enabling large-scale, virtual, and data-driven care models.

Her research focuses on artificial intelligence–enabled prediction of cardiovascular disease in cancer patients and survivors, health equity, and precision prevention. She has served on national scientific and advisory committees including NIH/NCI AI initiatives. She has been an invited speaker for the NCI Cancer Survivorship Lecture Series in collaboration with NHLBI and a moderator for the NCI Machine Learning and Health Outcomes in Cancer Care Delivery Research Workshops. She is Associate Editor for Artificial Intelligence on the Editorial Boards for Cardio-Oncology journal and American Heart Journal Plus. Dr. Brown remains an active national and internationally invited speaker on cardio-oncology, cancer survivorship, and AI-enabled cardiovascular care.

Dartmouth Cancer Center
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm New Hampshire in the American Revolution North Hampton Public Library and Cultural Center 239 Atlantic Avenue North Hampton NH 03862

Please join local historian Wendy Bergeron Kloc for "New Hampshire in the American Revolution"! This lecture will examine the first overt act of the American Revolution at Fort William and Mary, the extraordinary roles New Hampshire men played throughout the war, and the legacy that still remains over 250 years later.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchNorth Hampton Public Library and Cultural CenterEmily Dolan nhplprogramming@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:30 pm - 9:00 pm Nona Hendryx • Nightbirds: The Music of Labelle Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Groove on glam-funk from the past. Glimpse the future. Wear something silver.

When Labelle performed at the Met in 1975 as the first-ever African American female group, they redefined what Black femininity and artistic freedom could look like. Now, founding member Nona Hendryx brings her funk and Afro-futurist legacy to Dartmouth—radical, reimagined and more powerful than ever.

Joining her are two extraordinary voices: genre-defying powerhouse Kimberly Nichole and Alexia Jayy, the Alabama-born soul champion who made history as The Voice's first-ever Black woman winner. Together they bring Labelle's fearless songs to life—Lady Marmalade, Nightbirds, What Can I Do For You, Gypsy Moths and more. Labelle's music was always a prophecy. Come hear how it comes true again.

Hendryx is the 2026 Roth Visiting Scholar.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

Wednesday, July 1, 2026

11:00am What was it like to live in Tamworth in 1776? Cook Memorial Library 93 Main Street Tamworth NH 03886

What was it like to live in Tamworth in 1776? Travel to locations around town where young reenactors will share stories of the people and places that witnessed the founding of a new nation.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. 12:30 pm - 1:30 pm CURATOR-LED EXHIBITION TOUR Hood Museum of Art

Hood Museum curators will co-lead an exploration of some of the museum’s exhibitions and key themes highlighting the 250th anniversary of the United States. Drawing upon the museum’s collection of art and material culture, these exhibitions explore a range of subjects from sociological concerns such as immigration, labor, and revolution to art historical topics such as abstraction and Pop art. Space is limited. No registration is required. Please gather in Russo Atrium at least five minutes prior to the tour.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm US@250: Big Watch Film Screening & Discussion Lincoln Public Library 22 Church St Lincoln NH 03251

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence with a film screening and discussion!

 

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more. Expert facilitators will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future?
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history?
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?


Interested in watching the entire film series? Learn more here: The American Revolution | All Episodes Now Streaming | Ken Burns | PBS


Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS, with support from Cogswell Benevolent Trust. 

Corporate funding for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by Bank of America. Major funding was provided by The Better Angels Society and its members Jeannie and Jonathan Lavine with the Crimson Lion Foundation; and the Blavatnik Family Foundation. Major funding was also provided by David M. Rubenstein; The Robert D. and Patricia E. Kern Family Foundation; Lilly Endowment Inc.; and the 
following Better Angels Society members: Eric and Wendy Schmidt; Stephen A. Schwarzman; and Kenneth C. Griffin with Griffin Catalyst. Additional support for THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was provided by: The Arthur Vining Davis Foundations; The Pew Charitable Trusts; Gilbert S. Omenn and Martha A. Darling; Park Foundation; and the following Better Angels Society members: Gilchrist and Amy Berg; 
Perry and Donna Golkin; The Michelson Foundation; Jacqueline B. Mars; Kissick Family Foundation; Diane and Hal Brierley; John H. N. Fisher and Jennifer Caldwell; John and Catherine Debs; The Fullerton Family Charitable Fund; Philip I. Kent; Gail Elden; Deborah and Jon Dawson; David and Susan Kreisman; The McCloskey Family Charitable Trust; Becky and Jim Morgan; Carol and Ned Spieker; Mark A. Tracy; 
and Paul and Shelley Whyte. THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION was made possible, in part, with support from the Corporation for Public Broadcasting.

By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community-driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

 

Ample parking available.

Big WatchLincoln Public LibraryCarol Riley library@lincolnnh.gov 603-745-8159

Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm US@250: Big Watch Dunbar Free Library 401 Rt 10 South Grantham NH 03753


Be a part of something BIG: Join US@250: Big Watch, a statewide series of community-driven programs that bring Granite Staters together to watch, discuss, and reflect on the 250th anniversary of the Declaration of Independence and American Revolution.

 

Join us to commemorate and reflect upon the 250th anniversary with a film screening and discussion. Together, we’ll watch a short (30 min) excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein, and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. The featured segment from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION,“The Spirit of Service,” explores service as a core American value, from the nation’s founding through to the present day – examining volunteerism, civic participation, public leadership, and more.


Expert facilitator Mohamed Defaa will guide thoughtful conversations about “remembering together,” considering questions such as:

    • What moments from our past define who we are and point to a shared future? 
    • What are the key historical moments in your town’s or community’s history? 
    • How does focusing on these moments shape your sense of your community’s future?

 

This program will last approximately 1.5 hours (30 minutes for film screening, 1 hour for facilitated discussion). Please make sure you have appropriate technology for the film screening, including a strong internet connection, large screen, and sound system. Please contact programs@nhhumanities.org for the link to the themed excerpt. 

Big Watch programs are made possible by a partnership between New Hampshire Humanities and New Hampshire PBS.

 

"It's like being a part of history."
-Big Watch participant

Adult Education Program at Dunbar Free Library
Humanities to GoMohamed DefaaDunbar Free LibraryKatie Burns, 603-863-2172

Together, we’ll watch a short excerpt from THE AMERICAN REVOLUTION, a new film directed by Ken Burns, Sarah Botstein and David Schmidt, followed by a community discussion. Saturday, July 4, 2026

9:00am Monument Dedication Downtown Marlborough 28 Jaffrey Rd Marlborough NH 03455

On July 4 in front of the historic library building, we will gather to dedicate a new monument to Marlborough's Revolutionary War patriots. We will then gather to recreate a historic 1926 photo, in which the entire town was photographed in front of the library. This will be a great opportunity to show off the greater Marlborough community's identity in 2026!

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, July 5, 2026

7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Nichols Memorial Library 35 Plymouth St. Center Harbor NH 03226

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Please pre-register to attend by phone (603) 253-6950 or by email: centerharborlibrary@gmail.com on or before Friday, July 3rd.
Humanities to GoMary AdamsNichols Memorial LibraryLinda Verge, 6034841436 (Cell)
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, July 7, 2026

6:00pm Covered Bridges of New Hampshire - Past and Present Campton Public Library 1110 NH Route 175 Campton NH 03223

New Hampshire was once home to over 300 covered bridges. Today, there are over sixty authentic covered bridges in New Hampshire, forty-six of which are over a century old. These bridges exist solely because of the efforts of a small but powerful community that both recognized their significance and honored their tradition. Join author and photographer Kim Varney Chandler as she shares an overview of covered bridges in the Granite State, along with interesting facts she uncovered while researching her 2022 book, Covered Bridges of New Hampshire. Learn more about both existing and lost covered bridges in your area.

Humanities to GoKim Varney ChandlerCampton Public LibraryCampton Public Library, 603-726-4877
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:30 pm Deepfaking Sam Altman (DFS: Killer Robots!) Loew Auditorium, Black Family Visual Arts Center

A mordantly funny journey through technology, intimacy and accountability—where the filmmaker takes a page out of the AI playbook to deepfake his subject.

Director Adam Bhala Lough sets out to understand the technology and people at the center of AI. His quest sends him on a path towards OpenAI CEO, Sam Altman. When he can't sit down with Altman, Adam travels to India to create an AI version of him to interview instead. 

Sam Bot is a new kind of documentary subject and collaborator, who challenges Adam in surprising and hilarious ways. Together, Adam and Sam Bot explore what it means to be alive, create art and understand our era's obsession with simulation while longing for connection in an increasingly digital world.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Who Was John Stark? Booster Club 99 Main Street Ashland NH 03217

While many are familiar with the state motto “live free or die,” few might know about the man, General John Stark, who uttered these words. In this presentation, George Morrison reveals the man – a ransomed captive, Ranger officer, road-builder, lumberman, husband and father, repeatedly called-on to serve but often clashing with authority -- whose phrase has become synonymous with our state. 

Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonAshland Historical SocietySue Harville, sueharville@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Wednesday, July 8, 2026

9:00 am - 11:30 am What Can the Declaration of Independence Teach Us About AI? Lebanon Opera House 51 N Park St, Lebanonm NH 03766

Brinnae Bent is a faculty member and engineer at Duke University, where she directs the TRUST Lab and leads initiatives to make AI systems more explainable, ethical, and accessible. She has built machine learning algorithms for some of the world's largest companies and developed tools with real-world medical applications — from helping people walk to monitoring glucose noninvasively.

In her lecture, Dr. Bent uses an unexpected starting point — the Declaration of Independence — to explain how AI systems actually work, why they have the limitations they do, and what timeless questions about truth and legitimacy mean in an age of machine intelligence. No technical background required; this talk is designed for everyone.

NOTE: This lecture is part of a six-lecture series "How Is Artificial Intelligence Transforming America?" Six experts. Six Wednesdays. One of the most important conversations of our time. This July and August, the Osher at Dartmouth Summer Lecture Series brings nationally recognized voices on Artificial Intelligence to Lebanon, NH, and screens worldwide.

Cost:
Osher Member: $35
Non-Member: $45

Save by purchasing the full six-lecture series!
Osher Member: $135
Non-Member: $165

LEARN MORE AND REGISTER

We thank Merrill-Lynch Wealth Management for their support of this session.

Osher Lifelong Learning Institute at Dartmouth
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
5:30 pm - 7:00 pm Free Summer Concert: Dwayne Dopsie & the Zydeco Hellraisers Dartmouth Green

Explosive zydeco and soulful beats that will keep you on your feet.

Dwayne (Dopsie) Rubin hails from one of the most influential zydeco families in the world. Zydeco sits at the crossroads of Creole, Cajun, gospel and the blues with an upbeat energy rooted in Louisiana French accordion music and Afro-Caribbean beats. Inspired by tradition, Dwayne has developed his own high-energy style that blazes a refreshingly distinct path for 21st-century zydeco music. His music blends zydeco with R&B, funk, rock and roll, reggae and pop. This singer, songwriter and accordionist has performed all over the world since debuting his band, Dwayne Dopsie and the Zydeco Hellraisers, at age 19.

A 2022 Grammy Award recipient and Big Easy Award winner for Best Zydeco Band, Dopsie is known for his talent and magnetic stage presence, making him one of today's most dynamic and captivating performers.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗

5:40pm Declaration of Independence Reading Plymouth Town Hall 6 Post Office Square Plymouth NH 03264

The Declaration of Independence reading will be on the steps on the Plymouth Town Hall. A joint event between Pease Public Library, Plymouth Historical Society, and our local town government. This will be part of a nationwide simultaneous reading, commemorating the reading on July 8, 1776, in Phili. We will have people from all walks of life read. We’ll also have posters in the area and with our partners with questions about revolutionary actions & others to bring those questions in to the present, where people can add their ideas. 

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchPlymouth Historical Society, Pease Public Library, Plymouth Town GovernmentMarcia Schmidt Blaine info@plymouthnhhistory.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm What the Declaration of Independence Offers U.S. Social Movements Concord Public Library Auditorium 45 Green Street Concord NH 03301

The Declaration of Independence acknowledges a most volatile right: the people's right to rebel. If the people are not happy with their government, then it is within their unalienable rights to "alter and abolish it." In 1776, that right was exercised through armed rebellion. But is that the only way to exercise that right?

In 1963, John Lewis envisioned a different sort of revolution, one that would achieve the ends of freedom and liberty through non-violent means. In this "unfinished revolution," he argued, current enemies are seen as future friends. We'll consider how the language of the Declaration guides social movements, across the political spectrum, to institute a government that seems most likely to protect each other's unalienable rights.

Only street parking is available at the library. Metered parking is located within direct proximity to the library and free parking is available on Centre Street, between Rumford and Green street.
Humanities to GoMeg MottConcord Public LibraryJennifer Needham, jneedham@concordnh.gov
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Doris Benz Community Center 18 Heard Rd. Center Sandwich NH 03227

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Registration is not required but an email to sandwichlibrary@gmail.com letting us know your intention to attend and the number of attendees will be welcome & help with planning.
Humanities to GoMary AdamsSamuel H. Wentworth Library and Sandwich Historical SocietyNancy Fredrickson, 603-284-6665

Thursday, July 9, 2026

6:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Meredith Public Library 91 Main Street Meredith NH 03253

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

Parking is at the back of the building, off of High Street.
Humanities to GoMary AdamsMeredith Public LibraryErin Apostolos, erin@meredithlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:30 pm Summer Film: America Unfiltered Loew Auditorium, Black Family Visual Arts Center

This raw, resonant documentary road trip across the US asks what it means to be an American.

Over nine months, immigrant filmmakers Horacio Marquinez and Kirill Myltsev crisscrossed the United States—from desert outposts to the Capitol steps on January 6 to chaotic city corners—capturing the voices of everyday Americans. 

With a knack for striking up conversations in unlikely places, they speak with gun shop owners, conspiracy theorists, grieving mothers, undocumented workers, small-town locals and many others. The result: a textured, unvarnished portrait of a country reckoning with itself. America Unfiltered invites us to pause, listen—and find the unexpected threads that bind us.

Discussion follows. 

This screening and the accompanying photo exhibit in the Nearburg Forum (July 1-17) are programmed in collaboration with Dartmouth Dialogues.

 

This event is free and unticketed.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm New England's Colonial Meetinghouses and Their Impact on American Society Pembroke Town Library 313 Pembroke Street Pembroke NH 03275

New England's colonial meetinghouses embody an important yet little-known chapter in American history. Built mostly with tax money, they served as both places of worship and places for town meetings, and were the centers of life in colonial New England communities. Using photographs of the few surviving "mint condition" meetinghouses as illustrations, Paul Wainwright tells the story of the society that built and used them, and the lasting impact they have had on American culture.

Presentation will start promptly at 7 pm, so please arrive 10 min early. Parking on site. Light refreshments afterwards.
Humanities to GoPaul WainwrightPembroke Historical SocietySally Hyland, 603-566-1031
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Friday, July 10, 2026

7:00 pm - 8:30 pm Summer Film: The Animation Show of Shows Loew Auditorium, Black Family Visual Arts Center

This collection of wondrous, poignant and very funny animated shorts from around the world is back by popular demand.

The Hop has brought several showcases from the Animation Show of Shows, but the 18th edition got by us and features an array of highly creative, thought-provoking and moving works that reflect the filmmakers' unique perspectives. A delight for both families and adult fans of the art form, highlights include Disney/Pixar's Piper, the latest in 360º storytelling in Google's touching father-and daughter-journey the Oscar-nominated PEARL and About a Mother, a new folktale with echoes of Shel Silverstein's The Giving Tree, the show also features a late-night bonus of four provocative shorts exclusively for mature audiences.

Featuring techniques ranging from hand-drawn to stop-motion to the latest computer-generated imagery, the 17 extraordinary films in this program were created by animators from Belgium, Canada, France, Israel, Korea, Latvia, Norway, Russia, Scotland, the UK and the U.S. Their themes range from gay issues and civil rights, to deeply felt personal stories, to the wacky humor that animation does so well. 

Learn more about the lineup here.

Note: There will be a short break for children to leave before the final four films, which are for adults only.

 

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, July 11, 2026

6 am - 4 pm Hope returns. Bigger than ever Richmond Middle School, Hanover

Last year, you helped us go big.

Record-breaking community. Life-changing impact. Hope magnified. Thanks to your participation and support, we made history in the 2025 Prouty, benefiting Dartmouth Cancer Center.

This year? We’re going BIGGER.

In 2026, we’ve got our greatest opportunity to deliver the hope our cancer patients need and we can't do it without you.

[REGISTRATION NOW OPEN]

What’s making 2026 our biggest year yet? 

Now NINE ways to Prouty. Ultimate, Bike, Walk, Row, Golf, Volunteer, Community Walks, Virtual...and now Tennis joins the Prouty family!

An unprecedented match: The Byrne Foundation returns with a remarkable 1:1 donation match.

 

Our biggest goal: $12 MILLION to fuel ground-breaking research and important patient and family support services at Dartmouth Cancer Center. 

Registration is now open. Join us on July 10-11, 2026. 


Every mile you cover, every dollar you raise—gets us one step closer to a world without cancer.

 

Hope starts here. With YOU. 

[REGISTER TODAY]

**Register early and receive an exclusive Prouty gift! Be one of our first 500 registrants and score a Prouty neck warmer – perfect for your winter activities!**

Let’s go all in together. Let’s go BIGGER. 

The Friends of Dartmouth Cancer Center


Questions?
Email : info@theprouty.org

 

Dartmouth Cancer Center
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
10:30am Travel Back to 1776 Hooksett Public Library 31 Mt. St. Mary's Way Hooksett NH 03106

Travel back to 1776! Join us for a hands-on celebration of America's 250th birthday. Kids and adults alike can experience Revolutionary War life through interactive crafts, games, stories, presentations, and more! This event will also bridge the gap between the nation's founding and Hooksett's local heritage, exploring how our community has commemorated the American spirit throughout the centuries.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchHooksett Public LibraryJane Ayotte jayotte@hooksettlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
1:00pm How the Constitution Helps Us to Disagree Sandown Town Hall 320 Main St Sandown NH 03873

Deliberation is the practice that holds our democracy together, but does it always lead to better decisions? When citizens only talk to like-minded citizens, the thinking gets stuck and the decisions tend to be more extreme. This presentation gives participants a chance to find new ways to think about a contentious issue using the basic principles of the Constitution: freedom of speech and due process. Instead of carving out one’s (predictable) position in the culture wars, we’ll look at the best arguments on a contentious issue from the view point of progressives, conservatives, and libertarians. By the end of the discussion, participants will have a greater understanding of how the Constitution can help us to foster political friendships.

Humanities to GoMeg MottSandown Public LibraryTricia Thomas programming@sandownlibrary.us
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
2:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Kingston Historical Museum 169 Main Street Kingston NH 03848

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

The Kingston Historical Museum is not handicapped accessible at this time. Additional Parking is located at the Kingston Town Hall 163 Main Street. I MADE AN ERROR REGARDING THE DATE IN MY PREVIOUS APPLICATION FOR IS PRESENTATION. SO SORRY FOR THE CONFUSION.
Humanities to GoMary AdamsFriends of the Kingston Historical Museum AssociationLesley Hume, kingstonmuseumfriends@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm A Founder and a Framer Folsom Tavern 1 Governors Ln Exeter NH 03833

Titled “A Founder and a Framer,” this program will take place in the Folsom Tavern and feature physical artifacts, presentation, and facilitated discussion about the role of two of New Hampshire’s signatories to the Declaration (William Whipple) and the Constitution (Nicholas Gilman). Through the lens of these two lives, participants will be asked to consider these two documents, their epochal impact on American political life, as well as the events between 1776 and 1787 that linked the two.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchMoffatt-Ladd House & Garden MuseumNan Bulger director@moffattladd.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 9:30 pm 2001: A Space Odyssey (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Stanley Kubrick's dazzling masterwork set the gold standard for the human vs. machine conflict. 'Open the pod bay doors, HAL.'

Director Stanley Kubrick redefined the limits of filmmaking with this dazzling space opera—and with Christopher Nolan's "unrestored" version of the original camera negative, audiences can now see this landmark film with its original color palette, as close to Kubrick's intention as possible.

In a brilliant meditation on humans and their place in the universe, Kubrick visits our prehistoric ape-ancestry past, then leaps millennia into colonized space, and ultimately whisks astronaut Bowman (Keir Dullea) into uncharted space, perhaps even into immortality. Co-written by Kubrick and sci-fi visionary Arthur C. Clarke, this masterwork set the gold standard for the human vs. machine conflict—and is yet to find a cinematic equal. 

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Monday, July 13, 2026

6:30pm US@250: Unpacking the Declaration Independence: What It Meant Then and Now Dover Public LIbrary 73 Locust St. Dover NH 03820

Learn about the text of the Declaration of Independence, who the key players were, the Enlightenment influence and if the values espoused in the Declaration are still relevant today. Participants will experience an unbiased look at the Declaration and receive valuable content to understand the influences and ideals of the document. They will be able to critically think about the document and have the knowledge of what it actually says. They will be able to form opinions and speak with conviction about what they learned.

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

Free parking is available in the lot behind the library. Elevator access to the top floor Community Room.
Come and visit the newly renovated library!

Big WatchDavid AlcoxDover Public LibrarySusan Dunker s.dunker@dover.nh.gov

Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, July 14, 2026

9 am - 3 pm Course Design Institute Day 1 - July 2026 audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Public, Students-Graduate via Zoom

Dartmouth's Course Design Institute (CDI) provides a foundation in an integrated course design approach that is rooted in student-centered teaching and learning. Participants gain exposure to current theory of course design and, with support from a cohort of peers and guidance from CDI facilitators, apply evidence-based approaches to the designs of their own courses. This process can be used to develop a new course or redesign an existing course. This live institute will take place via Zoom July 14 and 16, 2026 from 9:00 am-3:00 pm.

Apply by June 15!

Faculty, Postdoc, Public, Students-GraduateDartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL), Learning Design and Innovation
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
10:00am New England Lighthouses and the People Who Kept Them Marion Gerrish Community Center 39 W Broadway Derry NH 03038

Everyone knows that there's "something about lighthouses" that gives them broad appeal, but their vital role in our history and culture is little appreciated. Our early nation was built on maritime economy, and lighthouses were part of the system that made that possible. Due to automation, traditional lighthouse keeping is a way of life that has faded into the past. Jeremy D'Entremont tells the history of New England's historic and picturesque lighthouses primarily focusing on the colorful and dramatic stories of lighthouse keepers and their families.

The Center is fully accessible, featuring an exterior side entrance ramp, an interior elevator, and an accessible elevator on the first floor. Both onsite parking and street parking are available for visitors.
Humanities to GoJeremy D'EntremontMarion Gerrish Community CenterDenise Barker, 603-434-8866 x2
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Shot NOT Heard 'Round the World Minot-Sleeper Library 35 Pleasant St Bristol NH 03222

An America 250 Event! Did you know that the American Revolution did not really begin with the famous shot "Heard 'Round the World" at Lexington-Concord in April 1775, but at Fort William and Mary in December 1774 in Portsmouth, NH?  Join Patriot re-enactor and amateur historian Michael Geanoulis as he sets the historical record straight and discusses the "Shot NOT Heard 'Round the World".

About Michael Geanoulis and more information on the assault on Fort William and Mary.

While Mr. Geanoulis holds a business degree from New Hampshire College and an associate degree with honors from DeVry Technical Institute, he rather hopes to be seen, instead, as a born-again amateur historian, author and poet, because of what he discovered in the forgotten pages of the British Archive File C05-939 imported from London.

     This historical record, with its detailed description of the 1774 assault on Fort William and Mary, not only elevates respect for the unsung heroes who stood against King George III’s rule but also illustrates their intent to overthrow the King’s authority to legislate entirely—not merely to seek repeal or protest unjust laws, as seen during the Tea Party or Gaspee Affair. The British file, also known as the Wentworth Papers, thus documents the first organized assault against the authority and property of King George III. It challenges the prevailing belief that the revolution began in Massachusetts in 1775, asserting instead that it began four months earlier with Paul Revere’s Ride to New Hampshire in 1774.

Big WatchMinot-Sleeper Library and Bristol Historical SocietyPatricia Hayward librarian@bristolnh.gov
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:30pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Conway Public Library 15 Greenwood Ave. Conway NH 03818

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Presentation will be held on the first floor of the library in the Periodical Room.
Humanities to GoMary AdamsConway Public LibraryHeather Corrigan, hcorrigan.conwayhistorical@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm The Hidden History of Lake Winnipesaukee Gafney Library 14 High Street Sanbornville NH 03872

Thought you knew all there was to know about the "Big Lake"? Think again! Lake Winnipesaukee's history goes well beyond the boating and other recreational-related activities we think of today. Join Glenn as he reveals the many facets of this history, including the real story about “America’s Oldest Summer Resort” in Wolfeboro, the religious sect on Gilford’s Governors Island who thought the world was ending, Massachusetts’ early efforts to claim New Hampshire territory as their own, the site of the country’s first college sports rivalry in Center Harbor, the African American community that once existed in Gilmanton, and the only airport of its kind in the entire country in Alton.  Whether you live in the area, are a visitor from elsewhere in the Granite State, or are a summer resident or tourist, these fascinating and enlightening bits of history will change forever how you view New Hampshire’s "Big Lake."

The program will be held in the Community Room of the Gafney Library, 14 High Street, Sanbornville. it is free and open to the public. Free parking is across High Street from the Library. The room is on the lower level and handicapped accessible by elevator.
Humanities to GoGlenn KnoblockWakefield-Brookfield Historical SocietyJennifer Rich, jbnr347@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Wednesday, July 15, 2026

4 pm - 5 pm The Ida Mae Specker Band: Square Dance Workshop Dartmouth Green

Join us for a lively afternoon of music and movement.

Learn traditional American social dances, including contra dances from New England, square dances from the Southern Appalachians, and reels with roots in Ireland and Scotland. 

Gibson teaches gender-free dances (traditionally, participants had either a 'lady' or 'gent' role), ensuring that all are welcome. 

The event is open to first-time dancers of all ages.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
5:30 pm - 7:00 pm Free Summer Concert: The Ida Mae Specker Band Dartmouth Green

Fiddle-fueled American folk comes alive.

Born into a family of "Vermont Folk Heroes" (Vermont Public Radio), third-generation fiddle player Specker draws on American folk tradition and drives it forward with her original work. 

Ida Mae Specker's band is a group of Vermont musicians, which includes legendary fiddler John Specker, Marc Edwards on guitar, Mowgli Giannitti on upright bass and Hank Clark on mandolin.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. 6:30pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Oscar Foss Memorial Library 111 South Barnstead Road Barnstead NH 03225

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:45 pm Ex Machina (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Domhnall Gleeson, Oscar Isaac and Alicia Vikander star in this AI thriller, where a young programmer is caught in a lethal game of manipulation between creator and creation.

Caleb Smith (Gleeson), a programmer at an internet-search giant, wins a competition to spend a week at the private mountain estate of the company's brilliant and reclusive CEO, Nathan Bateman (Isaac). Upon his arrival, Caleb learns that Nathan has chosen him to be the human component in a Turing Test—charging him with evaluating the capabilities, and ultimately the consciousness, of Nathan's latest experiment in artificial intelligence. That experiment is Ava (Alicia Vikander), a breathtaking AI whose emotional intelligence proves more sophisticated—and more deceptive—than the two men could have imagined.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Friday, July 17, 2026

7 pm - 10 pm National Theatre in HD: Les Liaisons Dangereuses Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Lesley Manville and Aidan Turner star in this thrilling game of love, lies and social warfare.

Marianne Elliott directs a striking new adaptation of Choderlos de Laclos' classic novel set in the glittering salons of the super-rich, where patriarchy equals power, reputation is everything—and for women, one misstep can mean ruin.

Marquise de Merteuil (Lesley Manville), master in the art of survival, wields her influence with intelligence and control. Alongside the magnetic Vicomte de Valmont (Aidan Turner), they turn seduction into strategy and weaponise desire. But when their alliance collapses into rivalry, the battle between them threatens to destroy everyone in their path.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, July 18, 2026

10:30am How the Constitution Helps Us to Disagree Peterborough Town Library 2 Concord Street Peterborough NH 03458

Deliberation is the practice that holds our democracy together, but does it always lead to better decisions? When citizens only talk to like-minded citizens, the thinking gets stuck and the decisions tend to be more extreme. This presentation gives participants a chance to find new ways to think about a contentious issue using the basic principles of the Constitution: freedom of speech and due process. Instead of carving out one’s (predictable) position in the culture wars, we’ll look at the best arguments on a contentious issue from the view point of progressives, conservatives, and libertarians. By the end of the discussion, participants will have a greater understanding of how the Constitution can help us to foster political friendships.

Peterborough Town Library is fully accessible. There is no pre-registration for this event. Please note that library parking is not guaranteed. Patrons who are able are encouraged to take advantage of Peterborough’s walkable downtown by parking in the Depot Square or Riverwalk lots.

Humanities to GoMeg MottPeterborough Town LibraryRebecca Enman renman@peterboroughnh.gov
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:45 pm The Terminator (DFS: Killer Robots!) Loew Auditorium, Black Family Visual Arts Center

Arnold Schwarzenegger embodies the definitive killer robot in James Cameron's masterful 1984 sci-fi action thriller.

Disguised as a human, a cyborg assassin known as a Terminator (Arnold Schwarzenegger) travels from 2029 to 1984 to kill Sarah Connor (Linda Hamilton). Sent to protect Sarah is Kyle Reese (Michael Biehn), who divulges the coming of Skynet, an artificial intelligence system that will spark a nuclear holocaust. Sarah is targeted because Skynet knows that her unborn son will lead the fight against them. With the virtually unstoppable Terminator in hot pursuit, she and Kyle attempt to escape.

The gladiatorial arena is set, with vulnerable flesh and cunning versus a leviathan who totes around massive weapons like so many chic accessories and can rebuild his organs as they get shot away. The pacing and the action are terrific, the future visions of a wasted LA are haunting, and Arnold's performance as one of cinema's greatest villains is so iconic that this character has become shorthand for what we fear artificial intelligence will become.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, July 19, 2026

7:30 pm - 9:00 pm Rhiannon Giddens Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

A roots music visionary reclaims the hidden stories of American sound.

Few artists move through musical worlds as boldly as Rhiannon Giddens. A singer, banjo virtuoso and musical storyteller, Giddens brings deep history to life with electrifying performances that connect past and present. From folk traditions to opera, her work shines a light on the overlooked voices that shaped American music and invites audiences to hear those roots in a whole new way.

The peerless Giddens returns to the Hop after leading a sold-out concert in 2022 as Artistic Director of the Silkroad Ensemble.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Monday, July 20, 2026

7:00pm The Finest Hours: The True Story Behind the U.S. Coast Guard's Most Daring Sea Rescue Campton Historical Society Museum 529 NH Rt 175 Campton NH 03223

On February 18, 1952, an astonishing maritime event began when a ferocious nor'easter split in half a 500-foot long oil tanker, the Pendleton, approximately one mile off the coast of Cape Cod, MA. Incredibly, just 20 miles away, a second oil tanker, the Fort Mercer, also split in half. On both tankers men were trapped on the severed bows and sterns, and all four sections were sinking in 60-foot seas. Thus began a life and death drama of survival, heroism, and a series of tragic mistakes. Of the 84 seamen aboard the tankers, 70 would be rescued and 14 would lose their lives. Michael Tougias, co-author of the book and Disney movie The Finest Hours, uses slides to illustrate the harrowing tale of the rescue efforts amidst towering waves and blinding snow in one of the most dangerous shoals in the world.

Humanities to GoMichael TougiasCampton Historical SocietyNancy Mardin mardinnancy@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Tuesday, July 21, 2026

9 am - 3 pm Gen AI Teaching Institute - Day 1 audience: Faculty, Postdoc Zoom

Are you wondering about how to transform your teaching practices in the era of Generative AI? Are you curious about the capabilities of different AI tools? Are you looking for an opportunity to collaborate with other colleagues grappling with the same challenges? This interactive workshop offers a tailored approach to integrating generative AI into your courses. Join us to explore and think through the implications of GenAI for your specific course, and adapt your design accordingly with the support of facilitators and feedback from peers. This is a two-day event (July 21 and July 23).
Apply by June 26

Faculty, PostdocDartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL), Learning Design and Innovation
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Range the Wild Woods Over: Loggers' Songs and Stories Minot-Sleeper Library 35 Pleasant Street Bristol NH 03222

Loggers at the turn of the twentieth century cut the timber that built and warmed our houses and provided the ties for America's ever-expanding railroads. Timber established Portsmouth, Portland and Bangor as important ports, sending New England lumber around the world. Folklorist Jeff Warner relates the songs and stories of the people who worked the wintry woods, showing their humor and their grit, and giving us a glimpse into everyday life in long ago lumber camps. 

Accesible parking lot on the west side of the library. Automatic doors, ramp, accesible restroom.
Humanities to GoJeff WarnerMinot-Sleeper LibraryPatricia Hayward, librarian@bristolnh.gov

Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. Add to Google Calendar
6:30pm Banjos, Bones, and Ballads Byron G Merrill Library 10 Buffalo Rd Rumney NH 03266

Traditional songs, rich in local history and a sense of place, present the latest news from the distant past. They help us to interpret present-day life with an understanding of the working people who built our country. Tavern songs, banjo tunes, 18th century New England hymns, sailor songs, and humorous stories about traditional singers and their songs highlight this informative program by Jeff Warner. 

Our room is fully accessible with plenty of free parking on site.
Humanities to GoJeff WarnerByron G Merrill LibraryKaty Gautsch, katy@rumneylibrary.org

Add to Google Calendar
7 pm - 9 pm Celebrating 35 Years of NYTW at Dartmouth Top of the Hop, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Join us to toast the prolific residency alongside the theater makers that bring underheard stories to the stage and find brilliant new ways to move us.

Let's welcome New York Theatre Workshop back to their summer home at the Hop and cheer on the future of innovative theater! Hear from the Pulitzer Prize-winning playwright Lynn Nottage and the boundless Nona Hendryx about their theatrical collaboration in the works.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm The Mammoth Road: New Hampshire Folk Tales as an Avenue to Local History and Culture Lake Winnipesaukee Museum 503 Endicott Street North (Route 3) Laconia NH 03247

Folk tales are passed from one generation to another, embellished by time and teller: “Ethan Crawford was as tall as Mount Washington. He could carry at least seven men on his back!” Yet, when we look at folk tales, the people who compiled them and the location from which they spring, we can access history in a new and entertaining way. Join us to hear and explore folk tales collected by the New Hampshire Women’s Federation (1932) and dig into local resources in search of truths. Was it tall tale or town history?

Reservations recommended as seating is limited
Humanities to GoErin MoultonLake Winnipesaukee MuseumBrendan Smith, lakewinnipesaukeemuseum@gmail.com

Thursday, July 23, 2026

9 am - 3 pm Gen AI Teaching Institute - Day 2 audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Public Zoom

Are you wondering about how to transform your teaching practices in the era of Generative AI? Are you curious about the capabilities of different AI tools? Are you looking for an opportunity to collaborate with other colleagues grappling with the same challenges? This interactive workshop offers a tailored approach to integrating generative AI into your courses. Join us to explore and think through the implications of GenAI for your specific course, and adapt your design accordingly with the support of facilitators and feedback from peers. This is a two-day event (July 21 and July 23).
Apply by June 26

Faculty, Postdoc, PublicDartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL), Learning Design and Innovation
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
5:30pm Covered Bridges of New Hampshire - Past and Present Gilford Public Library 31 Potter Hill Rd Gilford NH 03249

New Hampshire was once home to over 300 covered bridges. Today, there are over sixty authentic covered bridges in New Hampshire, forty-six of which are over a century old. These bridges exist solely because of the efforts of a small but powerful community that both recognized their significance and honored their tradition. Join author and photographer Kim Varney Chandler as she shares an overview of covered bridges in the Granite State, along with interesting facts she uncovered while researching her 2022 book, Covered Bridges of New Hampshire. Learn more about both existing and lost covered bridges in your area.

Humanities to GoKim Varney ChandlerPatty Myers Adult Outreach CoordinatorPatty Myers, patty@gilfordlibrary.org
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
6:00pm Henry Dearborn - From Captain to Cabinet Secretary Fuller Public Library 29 School Street Hillsboro NH 03244

In this presentation, George Morrison explores the life and career of Henry Dearborn, born in North Hampton, New Hampshire in 1751. He began his career as a young doctor before joining the the Contintental Army, where he served for over eight years. Post-war he served two terms in Congress, later as a cabinet secretary, and was, before retiring in 1824, President Monroe’s Minister to Portugal. But in 1818 he found himself in the middle of a controversy that would extend to 1843. Would you rather be elected governor, or insist on telling the truth? 

Parking across the street. Pre-register with the library 603-464-3595. Handicap accessible in the rear of the building, elevator available.
Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonFuller Public LibraryFuller Public Library, 603-464-3595
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 9:30 pm Hop Film Event: The American Revolution (with Ken Burns in person) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Ken Burns presents Episode Two from his newest documentary series, which examines how America's founding turned the world upside-down. 

Thirteen British colonies on the Atlantic Coast rose in rebellion, won their independence and established a new form of government that radically reshaped the continent and inspired centuries of democratic movements around the globe.

An expansive look at the virtues and contradictions of the war and the birth of the United States of America, the film follows dozens of figures from a wide variety of backgrounds. Through their individual stories, viewers experience the war through the memories of the men and women who experienced it: the rank-and-file Continental soldiers and American militiamen (some of them teenagers), Patriot political and military leaders, British Army officers, American Loyalists, Native soldiers and civilians, enslaved and free African Americans, German soldiers in the British service, French and Spanish allies, and various civilians living in North America, Loyalist as well as Patriot, including many made refugees by the war. 

These stories come to life through reenactments filmed across New England in every season, as well as paintings, letters, lithographs and other archival materials, from museums, galleries and libraries. Narrated by Peter Coyote, The American Revolution includes the first-person voices of nearly 200 individual historic figures, read by a star-studded cast including Meryl Streep, Morgan Freeman, Tom Hanks, Amanda Gorman, Kenneth Branagh and dozens more. The film also uses a wide variety of music, both from the period and newly composed pieces for the series, with recordings by Johnny Gandelsman, Rhiannon Giddens, Yo-Yo Ma and many more.

The Revolution began a movement for people around the world to imagine new and better futures for themselves, their nations and for humanity. It declared American independence with promises that we continue to strive for. The American Revolution opened the door to advance civil liberties and human rights, and it asked questions that we are still trying to answer today.

Discussion follows with filmmaker Ken Burns.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Flanders-Osborne Museum 74 Main Street Sunapee NH 03782

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. The slides and stories she chose were perfect for the subject and we could tell she knows a lot more than she could cram into the presentation. Mary is a valuable resource for learning NH history."

- Previous HTG host

Humanities to GoMary AdamsSunapee Historical SocietyBecky Rylander, 603-763-9872; sunapeehistory@gmail.com
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00pm Corbin’s Animal Garden Madison Library 1895 Village Road Madison NH 03849

In the late 1800s, the banking, railroad, and real estate mogul Austin Corbin returned to his hometown in Newport, New Hampshire. He built a grand estate and bought out his neighbors’ farms to create an 22,000 acre wildlife game preserve stocked with boar, bison, bighorn sheep, antelope, elk, Chinese pheasant, and other imported animals. The grounds eventually became a prestigious private hunting park and hosted illustrious guests including Theodore Roosevelt, the Prince of Wales, Cornelius Vanderbilt, Joe DiMaggio, Rudyard Kipling, and Augustus Saint-Gaudens.

This illustrated slideshow features archival images and a discussion of the complicated history and legacy of New Hampshire’s own American Gilded Age robber baron. The talk will also highlight the important legacy of the role the Corbin family and park naturalist Ernest Baynes played in the saving of the American bison from extinction.

The Chick Room at Madison Library is ADA accessible with ample parking. No registration needed. Light refreshments will be served.
Humanities to GoMary KronenwetterMadison LibrarySloane Jarell, librarian@madison.lib.nh.us

Friday, July 24, 2026

4:00 pm - 4:45 pm A SPACE FOR DIALOGUE GALLERY TALK Hood Museum of Art

I Remember Who I Am When I’m with You

The museum’s Class of 1954 Intern Madeleine Saraisky ’26 will discuss her student-curated exhibition that examines a range of photographs and prints that depict many types of relationships to explore the idea of interpersonal witnessing as a worthwhile action, one that shapes the course of our lives and allows us to impact others in return.

Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7 pm - 9 pm Blade Runner (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Harrison Ford hunts down rogue androids, nearly indistinguishable from humans, in Ridley Scott's masterful 1982 sci-fi noir adaptation of Philip K. Dick's story.

Tasked with hunting down a set of rogue androids, "Blade Runner" Rick Deckard's (Harrison Ford) mission is simple—but the issues it raises are anything but. It covers notions of sentience, empathy and human rights, with iconic supporting performances, unmatched production design and a dreamy synth score. 

One of the most influential films of the past 40 years, a dazzling fusion of retro-noir and future paranoia, set in a decaying, rain-drenched Los Angeles dominated by Mayan pyramids of unimaginable wealth. This "Final Cut" is the most complete version of this undisputed masterpiece, representing 25 years of finessing Scott's vision—a cinematic experience not to be missed.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, July 25, 2026

9:00am Celebrate 250America Kids Day! Moultonborough Public Library 935 Whittier Highway Moultonborough NH 03254

Join us on Saturday, July 25, from 9:00 am to 12:00 pm at the Moultonborough Historical Society for a festive morning celebrating America’s 250th birthday! This family-friendly event features a variety of hands-on activities, including painting wooden spinning tops, crafting paper tricorn hats, and trying your hand at fishing for apples. Step back in time at the historic schoolhouse to practice your alphabet on mini chalkboards, or visit the photo booth to dress up in historical clothing. While you're there, stop by the library station to sign up for Summer Reading and enjoy refreshments like popcorn and mini cupcakes on the museum porch. We can't wait to see you there for this historic community celebration!

This event is made possible by New Hampshire Humanities, in partnership with NHPBS, with additional support provided by Cogswell Benevolent Trust. By the People: Conversations Beyond 250 is a series of community driven programs created by humanities councils in collaboration with local partners. The initiative was developed by the Federation of State Humanities Councils and the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
Big WatchMoultonborough Public LibraryJosianne Fitzgerald, adultservices@moultonboroughlibrary.org

1:00pm Redcoats & Rebels: New Hampshire and the American Revolution Marlow Town Library 12 Church St. Marlow NH 03456

New Hampshire often gets overlooked in the narrative of the American Revolution, overshadowed by its noisy neighbor to the south. Nowadays, few people know about Paul Revere’s first ride, which was to Portsmouth in December 1774 to warn the patriots that the British were coming to reinforce Fort William and Mary, five months before the Redcoats marched on Concord and Lexington. Nor do they know that two-thirds of the troops at the Battle of Bunker Hill were from New Hampshire. Most people are also unaware that New Hampshire’s Provincial Congress adopted the first state constitution in January 1776, making no mention of royal authority and essentially declaring independence from Great Britain six months before anyone else. And this is just the beginning of New Hampshire’s revolutionary story. This program is presented as part of the New Hampshire Historical Society's celebration of America's 250th.

"Mary was well prepared and is a wonderful educator and speaker. She knows her subject well and her enthusiasm was evident. 4:00 pm - 5:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Moonie Bentley Theater, Hopkins Center for the Arts

A solo show about lineage, love, motherhood and the grief that comes with it all.

Did you know that although you were carried in your mother's womb, the egg you came from was first formed in your grandmother's womb? Moonie didn't. This year, Moonie turns 30, and has decided to go on dates with 100 men this year. Today, she is going on her 88th. It is not because her Taiwanese mother forced her. Or because she is lonely. Or because she is afraid she won't know how to keep living when her mother dies. 

This event is free but tickets are required.

Get more info here.

This event is free but tickets are required.

Add to Google Calendar

Sunday, July 26, 2026

4 pm - 6 pm Her (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

In the near future, a lonely writer develops an unlikely but emotionally resonant relationship with an operating system designed to meet his every need.

Written and directed by Oscar-nominated filmmaker Spike Jonze, Her centers on Theodore Twombly (Joaquin Phoenix), a complex, soulful man who lives in a near-future Los Angeles. 

Twombly, who is heartbroken by the recent end of a long relationship, makes a living writing touching, personal letters for others. He becomes intrigued, however, with a new "advanced operating system" that promises to be intuitive and unique in its own right. After initiating it, he meets "Samantha," a bright female voice (Scarlett Johansson) who proves insightful, sensitive and even funny. Soon, their needs and desires flourish together and their friendship deepens into an eventual love. 

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

4:00pm A Visit with Abraham Lincoln Whitney Community Center 16 Black Mountain Road Jackson NH 03846

Abraham Lincoln, portrayed by Steve Wood, begins this program by recounting his early life and ends with a reading of the "Gettysburg Address." Along the way he comments on the debates with Stephen Douglas, his run for the presidency, and the Civil War.

4:00pm Who Was John Stark? Center Meeting House PO Box 50 Newbury NH 03255

While many are familiar with the state motto “live free or die,” few might know about the man, General John Stark, who uttered these words. In this presentation, George Morrison reveals the man – a ransomed captive, Ranger officer, road-builder, lumberman, husband and father, repeatedly called-on to serve but often clashing with authority -- whose phrase has become synonymous with our state. 

Humanities to GoGeorge MorrisonCenter Meeting HouseLea McBain, 603-763-5803

Tuesday, July 28, 2026

All day Motivational Interviewing 2 Day Training To be determined

All dates: Jul 28, Jul 30

https://forms.gle/5DKZPUcn6VV3pGmV9 (Registration)

Summer 2026 Motivational Interviewing Training—Please register by July 15.

The Dialogue Project: Motivational Interviewing Two-Day Workshop

Tuesday, July 28 and Thursday, July 30
9 a.m.–4:30 p.m. (morning refreshments available at 8:30 a.m.)

 

Location: on campus at Dartmouth College, exact location to be determined

The Student Wellness Center, with support from the Dialogue Project, offers this intensive, interactive (and fun!) two-day workshop aimed at building basic proficiency in Motivational Interviewing (MI). MI is a collaborative communication style for supporting individuals through growth and change.

 

MI trainings are named in Dartmouth's Commitment to Care strategic plan as “effective skill-building trainings that support high-quality mentoring, advising, and teaching.” 

This evidence-based approach has broad application for working with students (and others) and can be an instrumental tool for advisors, coaches, facilitators, and educators. The training is free and open to the entire Dartmouth community. Morning refreshments, snacks, and lunch will be provided.

 

Facilitated by Caitlin Barthelmes, member of the MI Network of Trainers and LB White of the Student Wellness Center. 

 *Please attend both days. We offer this training twice a year. If you can't commit to the full training this time, please plan to attend the event when it is offered in December 2026.

 

Student Wellness Center

Jul 28 Jul 30

Wednesday, July 29, 2026

6:30 pm - 7:30 pm LECTURE Howe Library and on Zoom

Hood Museum of Art and Howe Library are collaborating on this year’s national summer reading theme, “Unearth a Story,” with particular reference to paleontology and archeology. The Howe has chosen to read Otherlands: A Journey Through Earth’s Extinct Worlds, by Thomas Halliday. In connection with this theme, Hood Curator of Collections Ashley Offill will explore the instrumental role of archeology in forming many early museum collections and the legacies and lessons of archeological objects in the modern museum with reference to the ongoing exhibitions Stone, Sand, and Clay: Connecting Cultures in the Ancient Mediterranean and From Mastodon to Mosaic: Building an Academic Art Collection in America. This hybrid program is free and open to all. To attend via Zoom, please register here. 

Hood Museum of Art
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 8:30 pm Exhibition on Screen: Turner & Constable Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

This new documentary charts the legacy and rivalry of two of Britain's greatest painters alongside the groundbreaking Tate exhibition.

Born within a year of each other—and celebrating their own 250th along with America—J.M.W. Turner and John Constable both used landscape painting to reflect the changing world around them. Turner's blazing sunsets and sublime scenes from his travels and Constable's idealised depictions of beloved places from home whipped the public of the time into a frenzy of enthusiasm. Constable represents the very best of the old school of realism and pastoral nostalgia; Turner, an exciting new way of depicting emotion and dreamlike impressions. Critics compared their starkly different styles to a clash of 'fire and water.' 

Tate Britain brought these two greats together for a groundbreaking exhibition in London in 2026, and this documentary brings that remarkable showcase of "Rivals & Originals" to the big screen. Discover unexpected sides to both artists with intimate views of sketchbooks and personal items, as leading experts offer insights gleaned from seeing these greats side-by-side, as they so often were in life.

Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
Jul 29 – Aug 9, 2026 Rodgers & Hammerstein's OKLAHOMA!

The Summer Musical Theater Intensive Production of
Rodgers & Hammerstein's OKLAHOMA!

Music by Richard Rodgers
Book and Lyrics by Oscar Hammerstein II
Based on the play "Green Grow the Lilacs" by Lynn Riggs
Original Choreography by Agnes de Mille
Directed by Kevin David Thomas

The Rodgers and Hammerstein musical that launched the Golden Age of Broadway comes to Northern Stage! Set in the Oklahoma Territory in 1906, Oklahoma! explores the conflict and opportunity that arose as the country expanded west. A revolution for the artform, the show reflected political and social changes in a way no other musical had dared to before . Laurey’s journey of independence and agency immortalized by the iconic “Dream Ballet,” solidifies the show as a beacon of change, not only for musical theater, but for the country as a whole.

About the Summer Musical Theater Intensive:
Each summer, we hold our celebrated professional-level theater training program for students ages 12-18! Over the course of 5 weeks, students rehearse and perform a musical at the Barrette Center for the Arts, fully supported by a professional creative and design team from around the country. This program extends the professionalism of our main stage to our young actors, giving them the opportunity to collaborate with industry veterans on an adventurous, joyful and exceptional theatrical experience.

JULY 29 - AUGUST 9, 2026

Thursday, July 30, 2026

7:00 pm - 8:45 pm Summer Film: Sundance Shorts Tour Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

A showcase of seven specially curated shorts from the 2026 Sundance Film Festival, the preeminent launchpad for young and new filmmaking talent.

Audiences who crave a bit of that Sundance Film Festival creativity can enjoy a mix of fiction, documentary and animated shorts that are funny, sad, inspiring and full of strong characters. Driven by innovation and experimentation, the Short Film Program seeks out filmmaking's most original voices from around the world. Past alumni of the shorts program include Andrea Arnold, Lake Bell, Damien Chazelle, Jay and Mark Duplass, Debra Granik, Todd Haynes, Dee Rees, Taika Waititi and many others. 

Get more info and tickets here.

Add to Google Calendar
7:00 pm - 9:15 pm The Matrix (DFS: Killer Robots!) Spaulding Auditorium, Hopkins Center for the Arts

Keanu Reeves learns kung fu to take on Hugo Weaving and his digital henchmen in this 1999 cyber thriller about the threats and possibilities of an increasingly virtual world.

In retrospect, this 1999 action thriller seems terrifyingly prescient, with its vision of a future where humankind is enslaved by an elaborate digital façade, while a malevolent cyber-intelligence dominates the "real" world. Whoa.

Hacker Neo (Keanu Reeves) takes the red pill and reluctantly joins rebels Morpheus (Laurence Fishburne) and Trinity (Carrie Anne Moss) in their struggle against "the Matrix." But learning kung fu and how to dodge bullets might not be enough against the digital henchmen (Hugo Weaving) determined to protect the system—it might require a completely new understanding of what is "real." 

 Get more info and tickets here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar

Saturday, August 1, 2026

4:00 pm - 5:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: The Punchline Bentley Theater, Hopkins Center for the Arts

If we only had a year to live, what would change in us?

The Punchline, a new solo piece from playwright and performer Christopher Rivas (The Real James Bond...Was Dominican), urges us to build an altar to our current life and the moments that touch and change us, while we're still alive. Through humor, story and ceremony, The Punchline reminds us to live in the present and celebrate it as sacred—because nothing can last forever.

World Premiere at City Theatre in Pittsburgh in May 2027

This event is free but tickets are required.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts
Full details ↗
Add to Google Calendar
7:30 pm - 9:30 pm New York Theatre Workshop: Los Feliz Daryl Roth Studio Theater

Two Latina mothers form a bond. Can it withstand the differences that divide them?

Irene and Emilia quickly connect over common ground: they're both Latina, working mothers, against giving soda to toddlers, and could use a helping hand. When Irene hires Emilia as a live-in nanny, Emilia gains Irene as an ally in her immigration process. Housed under one roof, they become company to each other's needs, wants and dreams. As their lives intertwine, can they transcend the divergent realities they were born into and now face?

This event is free but tickets are required.

Get more info here.

Hopkins Center for the Arts

Tuesday, August 4, 2026

9 am - 12 pm New Faculty Orientation to Teaching audience: Faculty, Postdoc, Students-Graduate Zoom

Dartmouth's New Faculty Orientation to Teaching provides useful information and introductions to help you get started successfully in your teaching role at Dartmouth. Facilitated by the Dartmouth Center for the Advancement of Learning (DCAL) and Learning Design & Innovation (LDI), the Orientation to Teaching is designed to complement other welcome and orientation sessions (with your department, the Dean of Faculty, and Office of Human Resources) that you might attend as you begin your work at the College. At Dartmouth's New Faculty Orientation to Teaching, participants will:

  • Discover resources and support for teaching
  • Hear from faculty and students about teaching and learning at Dartmouth
  • Meet people who can support your teaching
  • Meet other new faculty 
  • Get excited and more relaxed about teaching at Dartmouth This event is intended for faculty new to Dartmouth each year, or who will begin teaching at Dartmouth in the academic year.

If you are unsure whether this event is suitable for you, please contact us to inquire at dcal@dartmouth.edu. This orientation will take place on Zoom. Please register to receive meeting details.

Register here. (opens new tab)

Aug 12 – Aug 16, 2026 Northern Stage In Concert: SWEENEY TODD

The Northern Stage In Concert Production of
SWEENEY TODD: THE DEMON BARBER OF FLEET STREET

music & lyrics by Stephen Sondheim
book by Hugh Wheeler
from an adaptation by Christopher Bond

Attend the tale of Sweeney Todd! Stephen Sondheim’s thrilling, darkly comic masterpiece comes to Northern Stage in a special In Concert presentation. Featuring one of the most electrifying scores in musical theater, Sweeney Todd: The Demon Barber of Fleet Street tells the infamous story of a wronged barber seeking revenge in the streets of Victorian London. This concert reading puts Sondheim’s unforgettable music front and center, performed live by an extraordinary cast for an electrifying theatrical event.
 
About Northern Stage In Concert:
Northern Stage In Concert is a new musical concert reading series celebrating the power of iconic scores, performed by top-tier professional artists in an intimate, stripped-down setting. These fully produced concert readings put the music front and center, inviting audiences to experience legendary shows in a bold, immediate way, where musicianship and storytelling take the spotlight. All performances take place in the Byrne Theater at the Barrette Center for the Arts.
 
AUGUST 12-16, 2026

5 pm - 6 pm HopStop Family Event: Build a Bird of Prey Sustainability Park, 6 School Street, Hanover

What makes a bird of prey? Let's find out!

Build a bird with a raptor toolbox filled with touchable artifacts. Through song and dance, discover how raptors hunt and pack a raptor lunchbox to learn what they eat. Round out this experience by meeting one of our tiny but mighty raptor ambassadors! 

The Hop is excited to partner with Hanover Parks and Recreation as part of their Picnic in the Park Series.
